contests.office_does: A Washington Supreme Court justice serves a six-year statewide term on the nine-member court of last resort, setting binding precedent on the state constitution and state law and overseeing lower-court administration.
contests.race_blurb: Justice Colleen Melody, appointed by Gov. Ferguson after Justice Mary Yu's retirement, defends the seat against two challengers running to her right — litigator Scott Edwards and family law attorney Laura Christensen Colberg — who both make the gubernatorial-appointment pipeline itself an issue.

candidates.summary: The incumbent justice since January 2026, a career civil rights litigator who led the Attorney General's Civil Rights Division and argues state courts must safeguard rights as federal law changes.
highlights: Argues state courts must safeguard rights in privacy, the workplace, housing, healthcare, and education, particularly amid federal change [S1]
Led the successful challenge to the first Trump travel ban, won the initial TRO against ending birthright citizenship, and litigated to preserve DACA for nearly 20,000 Washingtonians [S3]
Endorsed by every sitting justice, three governors, the attorney general, the Washington State Labor Council, and the Seattle Times [S1][S5]
Appointed by Gov. Ferguson, her former boss — the central criticism from both challengers; she defends judicial elections as accountable and has recused twice under impartiality standards [S4]

judicial.basis: Frames state courts as guarantors of rights in privacy, work, healthcare, and education amid federal change, backed by a career of civil rights enforcement.

experience.basis: The sitting justice with sweeping institutional backing, though her tenure began January 2026 and her pre-bench record is as a litigator, not a judge.

candidates.summary: A family law attorney with 18 years as a Snohomish County commissioner pro tem and arbitrator, running on constitution-as-written impartiality and fast-tracking family law appeals; recommended by the state Republican Party.
highlights: Says she would 'measure all laws against the Constitution as written' and cites 'zero history of partisan activism' [S1]
Runs against the appointment pipeline — 'owes no favors to any governor, legislator, or party' — and calls the incumbent's appointment a conflict of interest [S3]
Signature proposal is fast-tracking family law appeals, arguing delay harms children in crisis [S1]
Claims thousands of judicial decisions across 18 years of part-time quasi-judicial service; smallest fundraising in the race, majority self-funded [S3]

judicial.basis: Campaigns on measuring laws against the constitution 'as written,' non-partisan impartiality, and independence from a 'stacked' judiciary.

experience.basis: Runs explicitly outside the appointment pipeline against the institutional incumbent, though she claims deep part-time bench experience.

candidates.summary: A tax and constitutional litigator with 30-plus years of appellate practice, including a dozen-plus cases before this court, campaigning to apply the law as written and 'take politics out of the courtroom.'
highlights: Says the court's role is 'to apply the constitution and laws as written, not rewrite them' [S1]
Calls the current bench 'one of the most progressive courts in the country' and argues recent appointments let policy outcomes influence decisions [S3]
More than 30 years of litigation at all levels of Washington courts, largely tax and constitutional matters, plus two decades teaching at UW Law [S1]
Has never held judicial office; raised about $53K, a quarter of the incumbent's total [S3]

judicial.basis: Explicit as-written, depoliticize-the-court platform and criticism of the current court's progressive direction.

experience.basis: Never held judicial office and runs against the appointment pipeline, while resting his case on a long private appellate career.

contests.office_does: One of nine statewide-elected justices on Washington's court of last resort, deciding appeals that set binding precedent on the state constitution and statutes and overseeing administration of the lower courts.
contests.race_blurb: An open seat vacated by Justice Raquel Montoya-Lewis has drawn three sitting judges with sharply different paths: appellate Judge Mike Diaz, King County Superior Court Judge Jaime Hawk, and Mason County Superior Court Judge David Stevens. The race offers the cycle's clearest three-way judicial-philosophy spread, and the endorsement landscape splits between two Democratic-appointed judges and a Republican-recommended elected judge.

candidates.summary: Washington Court of Appeals judge (Division I) and former federal civil-rights prosecutor, twice appointed to the bench by Governor Inslee. He describes his judicial philosophy as "American pragmatism" and leads the race in fundraising and establishment endorsements.
highlights: Only candidate with appellate-bench experience: has authored 200+ Court of Appeals opinions after four years as a trial judge [S1]
Describes his judicial philosophy as "American pragmatism" — law must "meet the community where they are" [S3]
Founded the Civil Rights Unit at the Seattle U.S. Attorney's Office and helped lead Seattle police reform efforts [S1]
Endorsed by three former governors, the attorney general, and nine current and retired Supreme Court justices; self-reports "Exceptionally Well Qualified" ratings from multiple bar associations [S1]
Leads the field in fundraising with over $220,000 raised as of early July [S3]

judicial.basis: Explicitly espouses "American pragmatism" with law meeting "the community where they are," an adaptive, community-responsive approach; pamphlet stresses protecting rights and freedoms.

safety.basis: Professional formation is mixed: 10 years as a federal prosecutor, but focused on founding a civil-rights unit and leading Seattle police-reform work rather than conventional enforcement.

experience.basis: Sitting appellate judge with 200+ authored opinions, eight years across two benches, and the race's broadest establishment endorsement roster — the maximal proven-record profile.

candidates.summary: King County Superior Court judge appointed by Governor Inslee in 2022 and twice elected since, after roughly 20 years as a public defender and ACLU of Washington legal strategist. She is endorsed by sitting Governor Ferguson and more than 100 judges.
highlights: Says justices must apply the rule of law "based on our constitution, our statutes ... precedent ... not personal views," while remaining open to reform [S4]
First ACLU of Washington attorney to become a judge, after a career in state and federal indigent defense [S1]
Has presided over hundreds of criminal, civil, and family cases since 2022; her pamphlet states no trial has been overturned for legal error [S1]
Endorsed by Governor Ferguson, Justice Whitener, retired Justice Yu, and 100+ current and retired judges [S1]

judicial.basis: Stakes a middle framing of constitution, statute, and precedent over personal views, but pairs it with openness to reform and a rights-and-access emphasis rooted in her civil-liberties career.

safety.basis: Professional formation is squarely on the defense/civil-liberties side: ~20 years as a state and federal public defender and ACLU of Washington attorney; no explicit bail or diversion positions documented.

experience.basis: Sitting Superior Court judge since 2022, twice elected, with hundreds of cases presided — a proven-record profile, though with shorter bench tenure than Diaz or Stevens.

candidates.summary: Mason County Superior Court judge who reached the bench by election rather than appointment, after a career spanning prosecution, public defense, and international rule-of-law work in Kosovo and Afghanistan. He describes himself as a strict constructionist and holds Republican party recommendations.
highlights: Self-described "strict constructionist": statutes read literally, and the constitution interpreted "based on its text and original meaning and not on evolving societal standards" [S2]
Says public policy "is the province of the political branches and not the courts" and argues his election would bring balance to a court he sees as progressive-leaning [S1]
Career spans senior felony prosecution, federal public defense, tribal chief public defense, and international justice missions, with 200+ jury trials as lawyer and judge [S1]
Only candidate who reached the bench by election rather than gubernatorial appointment, which he makes a central argument [S1]
Recommended by the state, King County, and Snohomish County Republican parties; trails the field in fundraising [S3]

judicial.basis: Repeated explicit self-description as a strict constructionist committed to text and original meaning, judicial restraint, and courts staying out of policy-making.

safety.basis: Supports issuing warrants for defendants who fail to appear and opposes race-based jury selection, with law-enforcement (WACOPS) support; his mixed prosecutor/defender formation tempers the placement.

experience.basis: Long proven trial record (25+ years, 200+ jury trials, sitting elected judge) paired with an explicit outsider campaign against the appointment pipeline and the court's current direction.

contests.office_does: One of nine statewide-elected justices on Washington's court of last resort, deciding appeals, state constitutional questions, and the rules that govern every court in the state.
contests.race_blurb: A special election to fill retired Justice Barbara Madsen's term, and the most crowded of the 2026 Supreme Court races: Justice Theo Angelis, appointed by Governor Ferguson in March 2026, faces retired Federal Way Municipal Court Judge Dave Larson, Thurston County Superior Court Judge Sharonda Amamilo, and veteran appellate attorney Greg Miller. Voters are effectively choosing among appellate craft, long trial-bench tenure, and current trial-court judging, with the top two advancing.

candidates.summary: Sitting Supreme Court justice appointed by Governor Ferguson in March 2026 after 26 years as a K&L Gates appellate and IP partner, with extensive pro bono immigration work. He runs with endorsements from eight sitting justices and three governors but only months of bench record.
highlights: On the Supreme Court since April 4, 2026, with no authored opinions identified as of mid-July — roughly three months of bench record [S4]
26 years leading appellate practice at K&L Gates; pro bono litigation secured government-paid lawyers for children in immigration proceedings [S1]
Says he has "fought for the rule of law, the rights of all individuals" and courts that are "fair, independent, and accessible" [S1]
Endorsed by eight sitting justices, Governors Ferguson, Inslee, and Gregoire, and the Washington State Labor Council; leads the field in fundraising [S3]
Rated "Exceptionally Well Qualified" by Washington Women Lawyers [S6]

experience.basis: The sitting justice with a 26-year appellate career and near-unanimous institutional support, though his bench tenure is only about three months by appointment rather than election.

candidates.summary: Retired Federal Way Municipal Court judge with 18 years on the bench, making his fourth run for the Supreme Court after losing the 2024 race by under one percentage point. He runs as an explicit critic of the current court's direction, with the state Republican Party's recommendation and Forward Party support.
highlights: 18 years as an elected municipal-court judge (16 as presiding judge) plus 23 years as a civil trial lawyer; retained by voters four times [S3]
Calls the current court "a ship lost at sea," criticizes the Blake drug-possession decision as "motivated by ideology," and opposes proposed bail rules he says would cripple trial courts [S5]
Says judges should not legislate from the bench even when they personally disagree with a law [S4]
Served years on the DMCJA Therapeutic Courts Committee and describes trial-court approaches to crime and addiction combining "compassion and accountability" [S3]
Recommended by the Washington State Republican Party and endorsed by the Forward Party; second in fundraising at roughly $232,000 [S4]

judicial.basis: Repeated restraint-and-depoliticization themes — judges must not legislate from the bench, Blake was ideologically motivated, restore trust by rising above partisan interests — without an explicit originalist framework.

safety.basis: Genuinely mixed documented record: opposes proposed bail rules restricting warrant issuance, yet served six years on the Therapeutic Courts Committee and campaigns on combining compassion and accountability for crime and addiction.

experience.basis: Longest judicial tenure in the field (18 years, municipal level) but campaigns as an outside change agent against the current court and the appointment pipeline.

candidates.summary: Thurston County Superior Court judge, former 12-year public defender, and retired Army intelligence chief warrant officer, elected in 2020 as the first African American judge in Thurston County history. She emphasizes precedent, trial-court perspective, and carries the race's most extensive bar-association ratings.
highlights: Sitting superior-court judge since 2021 handling felony trials, complex civil, family, and dependency dockets, plus Court of Appeals pro tem work [S2]
Emphasizes adherence to precedent for stability, with a "very high bar" for revisiting it, and stresses textual precision in statutory interpretation [S4]
News coverage reports she is known for scrutinizing bail and sentencing recommendations, particularly where disparities appear racially motivated [S5]
Rated "Exceptionally Well Qualified" by five minority and specialty bar associations and "Well Qualified" by a sixth [S1]
Endorsed by Justice Whitener, former Justice Yu, labor organizations, and numerous Democratic organizations, but trails badly in fundraising at roughly $35,600 [S2][S3]

judicial.basis: Genuinely mixed placement: precedent-and-stability emphasis with textual precision ("Words matter") on one side, and access-to-justice, consistency, and child-and-family protection priorities on the other.

safety.basis: Twelve years as a public defender plus press reports that she scrutinizes bail and sentencing recommendations for racially motivated disparities.

experience.basis: Runs on a proven current trial-bench record — sitting superior-court judge since 2021 with weekly trial work and top bar evaluations — rather than an outsider case.

candidates.summary: Seattle appellate attorney and Carney Badley Spellman partner with a 40-year Washington appellate practice — over 135 appeals and 80 oral arguments — but no judicial office beyond pro tem service. He frames the race around irreplaceable appellate craft and independence from the appointment process.
highlights: 40-year Washington appellate practice: briefing in 135+ appeals, 80+ oral arguments, and 25 amicus briefs in the state Supreme Court [S1][S2]
Describes taking cases individually without predetermined conclusions and applying the law fairly; says he is "not in anyone's pocket" [S3]
Expresses concern that unchecked gubernatorial appointment power can undermine judicial independence [S3]
Has never held elected judicial office; judging experience is limited to Seattle Municipal Court pro tem service [S1][S2]
Lists no endorsements and has raised about $22,000, a small fraction of the incumbent's total [S2][S4]

experience.basis: Mixed profile: a deep 40-year professional appellate record is his central pitch, yet he has never held judicial office and runs as an independent outsider to the appointment pipeline with no endorsements.

contests.office_does: One of nine statewide-elected justices on Washington's court of last resort; this seat's incumbent additionally serves as Chief Justice, presiding over the court and leading administration of the state judiciary.
contests.race_blurb: The most asymmetric of the 2026 Supreme Court races: Chief Justice Debra Stephens, on the court since 2008 and author of the McCleary school-funding decision, faces three challengers — public-defender-turned-solo-attorney Karim Merchant, tax and business attorney Todd Bloom, and solo family/tax lawyer David Shelvey — none of whom has held judicial office. Institutional endorsements and fundraising are one-sided in the incumbent's favor.

candidates.summary: Sitting Chief Justice of the Washington Supreme Court, on the court since 2008 after Court of Appeals service and a career as an appellate attorney and Gonzaga law professor. She authored the landmark McCleary school-funding decision and runs on judicial independence and access to justice.
highlights: 18 years on the Supreme Court, currently in a second tenure as Chief Justice, including leading the judiciary's COVID-19 response [S4]
Authored the majority opinion in McCleary v. State (2012), holding that the constitution confers a positive right to an amply funded K-12 education [S5]
States her core commitments as "an independent judiciary, courts that are open and accessible to everyone, and decisions grounded in law, not politics" [S2]
Endorsed by all eight other sitting justices, five retired justices, more than 150 judges, Governor Ferguson, and legislative leaders [S3]
Holds the race's only substantial war chest (~$83,000); her ballot opponent reported zero contributions [S6]

judicial.basis: Verified author of McCleary, reading the state constitution as guaranteeing a positive, enforceable right to funded education; platform pairs that rights-protective record with law-not-politics framing.

experience.basis: Sitting Chief Justice with 18 years on the court, prior Court of Appeals service, a landmark opinion record, and endorsement of the entire remaining bench — the maximal proven-record case.

candidates.summary: Criminal defense attorney who spent 15 of his 18 career years as a King County public defender and now runs a solo practice. He challenges the Chief Justice on a follow-the-Constitution, anti-establishment platform with no endorsements and minimal fundraising.
highlights: States his judicial philosophy as "follow the Constitution": courts should interpret law as written rather than inserting subjective intent [S3]
Says the current court has an "establishment culture," "clearly leans left," and criticized justices' participation in Pride flag ceremonies as creating an appearance of prejudgment [S3]
Disagreed with the Quinn capital-gains-tax ruling but supported the Blake drug-possession decision as constitutionally sound [S3]
15 years as a King County public defender; says he has never represented corporations or the government [S1]
No judicial service, no endorsements, and about $9,800 raised; frames both as marks of independence [S1][S4]

judicial.basis: Repeated explicit as-written philosophy — interpret law without subjective intent, keep courts free of politics — plus criticism of the court's perceived left lean and Quinn, applied consistently even where it favors defendants (Blake).

safety.basis: Professional formation is 15 years of public defense focused on protecting people from unlawful government intrusion, and he supported the Blake decision striking the drug-possession statute.

experience.basis: No judicial service and no endorsements by design; explicitly frames his candidacy as challenging the establishment from outside the appointment and endorsement system.

candidates.summary: Tax and business attorney (CPA, CFA, CFP), former FINRA arbitrator, Navy veteran, and WSBA Board of Governors member challenging the Chief Justice. He argues justices owe loyalty to the Constitution rather than making law and carries the state Republican Party's recommendation.
highlights: Says judges owe loyalty to the Constitution and should defend it rather than make law, and frames the race as a choice to bring "meaningful new perspective" to balance the court [S3][S1]
Called the Quinn capital-gains ruling "wrongly decided," praising the dissent, and criticized the Blake drug-possession decision as undermining public trust [S3]
Two decades in tax, business, and securities law plus FINRA arbitration (1999-2020); no judicial service [S1]
Elected WSBA Board of Governors member (District 6) since 2023; 11 years as an active-duty Navy officer after 9/11 [S2]
Recommended by the Washington State Republican Party; minimal fundraising (~$4,700) [S4][S5]

judicial.basis: Constitution-loyalty, judges-should-not-make-law framing plus explicit criticism of both Quinn and Blake and a stated goal of balancing the court.

safety.basis: Criticized the Blake drug-possession decision, saying its outcome astonishes average citizens and undermines trust — an enforcement-side posture on the race's main criminal-law question.

experience.basis: No judicial service and a candidacy explicitly premised on bringing new perspective and common sense to the bench from outside the judiciary.

candidates.summary: Solo-practice attorney from Sumner handling family, estate, tax, and civil matters, making his second Supreme Court run after drawing 3.4% in a 2024 primary. His pamphlet statement takes unusually outcome-oriented positions for a judicial race and he reports no judicial experience and no campaign contributions.
highlights: States in his pamphlet that he opposes a state income tax and "won't be soft on criminals" — outcome-oriented positions unusual in a nonpartisan judicial race [S1]
Reports no judicial experience, which he characterizes as "the same as many lawyers have when they take to the bench" [S1]
Eleven years of solo practice spanning family law, estate planning, taxation, and civil litigation [S1]
Received 3.4% statewide in the 2024 Supreme Court Position 2 primary; deliberately lists no endorsements to preserve impartiality [S4][S3]
PDC records show no reported contributions for the 2026 cycle [S5]

experience.basis: Solo practitioner with no judicial experience, no endorsements, no reported contributions, and a prior 3.4% statewide run — the field's fullest outsider profile.

safety.basis: His pamphlet pledge that he 'won't be soft on criminals' is a direct, documented enforcement-side posture toward criminal defendants — far more probative for the safety axis than for judicial philosophy, where the scorer placed it. Single clear statement supports medium; flagged for the merger because it is a stated posture rather than the bail/diversion/formation evidence the guide enumerates.

contests.office_does: County commissioners adopt budgets and policy, oversee departments and infrastructure, and administer land-use, health, safety, and human services.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Richard Dahl, a retired banking executive, faces STEM teacher Chelsea Chandler; the approved sources provide little detailed policy evidence for either candidate.

candidates.summary: Chandler is a middle-school STEM teacher challenging for the District 3 commission seat; detailed policy positions remain an evidence gap.
highlights: Works as a STEM teacher at Monticello Middle School [S1][S2]

experience.basis: The dossier identifies a teaching career and no prior elected or county-administration tenure, making her the clear renewal candidate in this race.

candidates.summary: Dahl is the incumbent District 3 commissioner and a retired banking executive with 39 years of management experience.
highlights: Official county records verify his current commission service [S2]
His banking career included senior management over branches, lending, and a call center [S1][S2]

experience.basis: He combines verified incumbency with a 39-year banking-management career.

contests.office_does: The King County Assessor values roughly 750,000 properties annually and administers tax-relief exemptions and the valuation side of appeals; it distributes the levy burden but does not set tax rates.
contests.race_blurb: An open seat after incumbent John Arthur Wilson, facing criminal charges and a unanimous council resignation demand, declined to run again. The field splits into two insiders — Chief Deputy Assessor Al Dams and appraiser Rob Foxcurran — and two outsiders, Shoreline Councilmember Christopher Roberts and property-rights activist Dominique Scarimbolo, all campaigning on restoring trust.

candidates.summary: A small-business owner and Citizens Alliance for Property Rights chapter president running as a non-politician outsider, arguing the county's assessments are non-uniform in violation of state law and the state constitution.
highlights: Argues, from self-described independent research, that current assessments 'produce highly non-uniform results, violating both state law and our WA state constitution' [S1]
Says the growing property-tax burden is 'unsustainable' and has submitted multiple audit proposals targeting the property taxation system [S1]
Runs on taxpayer rights and due process from personal experience with the appeals process — 'I am proudly not a politician' [S1]
Mini-reporting PDC status indicates a low-budget campaign [S3]; rated 'Best Choice' by the King County Republican Party in this nonpartisan race [S5]

taxes.basis: Calls the property-tax burden 'unsustainable' and demands reform now; leads a property-rights organization chapter; proposes no new revenue tools.

spending.basis: Audit-first platform: multiple submitted audit proposals and a campaign centered on transparency, accountability, and taxpayer due process.

experience.basis: Campaigns explicitly as a non-politician outsider against a chief deputy, a certified appraiser, and a four-term councilmember.

candidates.summary: A four-term Shoreline councilmember and two-term mayor running on general-government leadership and a 'fresh start' for a troubled office, with the field's largest roster of elected-official endorsements.
highlights: Attributes the office's problems to a 'leadership crisis' at the top, not frontline staff, and proposes a citizen oversight committee [S1]
Would create a tribal liaison position [S1]; an enrolled Choctaw Nation member who would be the first Native American elected to King County government [S4]
Prioritizes clearing exemption backlogs, modernizing operations, and working with the Legislature on homestead exemptions [S2]
Endorsed by Executive Girmay Zahilay, former Assessor Lloyd Hara, Rep. Suzan DelBene, and more than 60 local elected leaders [S1]
Offers elected-executive and budget experience plus IAAO membership rather than hands-on appraisal practice [S1]

spending.basis: Cites budgetary mismanagement, proposes a citizen oversight committee, and lists 'Fiscal Responsibility' and cutting red tape as platform planks.

experience.basis: Runs on 16-17 years of elected office, complex budget management, and regional board leadership, though new to assessment administration.

candidates.summary: A Certified General appraiser, Seattle Senior Appraiser, and former county appeals-board hearing examiner running as a labor-backed reformer who argues the system lets corporate appellants shift the tax burden onto homeowners.
highlights: The only candidate with hands-on valuation work: certified appraiser and former Hearing Examiner on the King County Board of Appeals and Equalization [S1]
Would push the Legislature to lift the 1% levy cap, establish homestead protections, and create a renter's tax credit [S1]

Says corporations use appeals experts and lawyers to shift the tax burden onto homeowners, renters, and small businesses, and would assess large commercial properties as rigorously as residential [S2]
Endorsed by the Seattle Times editorial board [S5] and PROTEC17 [S6]; campaign-reported endorsements include the King County Democrats and ten LD Democratic organizations [S2]
Would use assessor data to identify surplus public land for affordable-housing development [S2]

taxes.basis: Explicitly backs lifting the 1% levy cap and shifting burden toward large corporate owners, paired with homestead and renter relief.

housing.basis: Would use assessor data and authority to identify surplus public land for affordable housing, plus a renter's credit and anti-displacement payment options.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record but deep professional credentials in the office's own field — a profession-insider, office-outsider profile.

candidates.summary: The office's Chief Deputy Assessor for 11 years, running on continuity, technical competence, and expanding exemption eligibility after the incumbent's legal troubles.
highlights: Eleven years as Chief Deputy, leading valuation of roughly 750,000 properties, with more than 30 years in King County government [S1]
Committed to expanding property-tax exemptions so people 'of all ages' are eligible, building on senior-exemption work [S1]
Frames the race as experienced in-office leadership versus candidates who 'must learn it from scratch' [S2]
Served as second-in-command under Assessor John Wilson; publicly stated no internal investigation into data-misuse allegations had occurred [S4]
Endorsed by individual officials including Councilmember Claudia Balducci; the Seattle Times endorsed opponent Foxcurran [S1]

experience.basis: The continuity candidate: 11 years as the office's second-in-command and 30-plus years in county government leadership roles.

contests.office_does: A U.S. Representative writes and votes on federal law in the House, originates tax bills, controls federal spending through appropriations, conducts oversight of the executive branch, and runs constituent services for the district.
contests.race_blurb: Seven candidates compete in the August 4 top-two primary: seven-term incumbent Suzan DelBene (D), four other Democrats, one Republican, and one independent. DelBene holds a large fundraising and endorsement advantage in a district that has favored Democrats in recent cycles, so the practical question is which challenger joins her on the November ballot.

candidates.summary: Seven-term U.S. Representative (since 2012) who serves on the House Ways and Means Committee and chairs the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee. A former Microsoft executive and director of Washington's Department of Revenue.
highlights: Has sponsored 153 bills since 2012, including an enacted infant-formula law [S4]; says she has led efforts to permanently expand the Child Tax Credit [S1]
Says she is focused on lowering the cost of living, reducing taxes for working families, and ensuring the wealthy pay their fair share [S1]
Introduced bipartisan legislation to spur creation of two million affordable homes [S1]
Supports immigration reform and says ICE agents must be 'reined in' [S1]
Endorsed by Sen. Patty Murray, Gov. Bob Ferguson, county Democratic parties, and the Washington State Labor Council [S3]

taxes.basis: Says the wealthy should pay their fair share and opposes the 2025 Republican tax law, while emphasizing tax cuts for working families and targeted credits rather than broad new wealth taxes.

spending.basis: Record of expanding programs — Child Tax Credit expansion push, affordable-housing legislation, opposing Medicaid and food-assistance cuts — within a targeted, bipartisan framing.

climate.basis: Sponsored the Clean Competition Act (carbon-based trade measure), supports renewable-energy investment including a regional hydrogen hub, and is endorsed by Sierra Club and League of Conservation Voters.

experience.basis: Fourteen-year incumbent with an enacted legislative record, a Ways and Means seat, and national party leadership as DCCC chair — the maximal proven-record profile in this field.

reform.basis: Chairs the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee and runs with broad establishment, party, and institutional-labor endorsements — coalition-building within existing party and donor structures.

immigration.basis: Supports immigration reform and says ICE agents must be 'reined in,' pairing reform with ICE accountability.

healthcare.basis: Works within the current system: lower premiums, roll back Medicaid cuts, and Medicare chronic-care and prior-authorization reform bills; no single-payer support in the dossier.

social.basis: Supports reproductive rights, opposes a nationwide abortion ban, and is endorsed by Planned Parenthood Action Fund.

tech.basis: Repeatedly sponsors data-privacy legislation (Email Privacy Act) while being a former Microsoft executive with a tech-industry-friendly legislative profile — evidence points both directions.

candidates.summary: First-time candidate who describes himself as a working American with a blue-collar job and a 'moderate reform Democrat' emphasizing practical solutions and pilot-tested programs over ideology.
highlights: Would eliminate federal income tax and filing requirements for Americans earning $61,000 or less [S1]
Supports a pilot program to test universal healthcare before any national rollout and opposes forcing an end to private health insurance [S1]
States 'men should not be in women's sports or women's prisons' and 'socialism doesn't work' [S1]
Says he takes no corporate PAC money and campaigns on outsider status against the incumbent's twelve-year record [S3]

taxes.basis: Signature proposal is a large working-class tax cut (no federal income tax under $61,000) rather than new progressive revenue.

spending.basis: Frames homelessness policy around 'real accountability rather than endless spending with no results' and says the defense budget can spend less through efficiency.

safety.basis: Accountability-first framing on homelessness and crime, with a Secure Storage Law targeting stolen firearms that fuel street crime and victim-protection accountability acts.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record who explicitly campaigns on outsider, blue-collar status against career politicians.

reform.basis: Refuses corporate PAC money and centers his rationale on scrutiny of the incumbent's tenure and wealth growth in office.

healthcare.basis: Open to universal coverage but only via a pilot-tested public-option approach, and opposes ending private health insurance — genuinely between the poles.

social.basis: States men should not be in women's sports or prisons and runs a 'women's safety and fairness' sex-based-spaces agenda, positioning right of the incumbent on gender issues.

tech.basis: Calls for accountability from major tech companies on layoffs and H-1B use while treating AI-era energy demand as a housing/manufacturing opportunity — evidence in both directions.

defense.basis: Supports Ukraine, Taiwan, and a free Iran with zero tolerance for antisemitism (alliance commitments), tempered by a call for defense-budget efficiency.

candidates.summary: Paraeducator in the Marysville School District and developmentally disabled self-advocate who has experienced homelessness; a repeat candidate whose platform centers disability policy and structural economic change.
highlights: Supports Medicare for All, framing healthcare as a human right [S1]
Supports a universal basic income, a federal jobs guarantee, and nationalizing AI and automation [S1]
Wants to remove income caps on Social Security disability income and increase funding for paraeducators and home care [S2]
No FEC registration or reported fundraising found as of mid-July 2026 [S3]

taxes.basis: Calls for 'taking on extreme wealth,' raising corporate tax rates, and taxing AI/automation companies to fund programs.

spending.basis: Would fully fund Section 8, build large-scale affordable housing, expand disability and home-care funding, and add UBI and a federal jobs guarantee — public investment as the primary lever.

climate.basis: Supports environmental regulations and climate-adjacent aid for disabled and low-income families, while describing climate as a lower campaign priority.

experience.basis: No elected experience (prior primary losses for state House and city council); runs on lived experience of disability, homelessness, and poverty rather than institutional tenure.

immigration.basis: Says ICE is overfunded and wants to support immigration as much as possible while still policing criminal activity.

healthcare.basis: Supports Medicare for All / universal healthcare, opposing insurance-profit-driven care.

tech.basis: Would nationalize AI and automation so the technologies benefit the public, and tax AI/automation companies — the maximal intervention position in this field.

candidates.summary: Hardware engineer and Monroe School District board director running as the race's sole independent. Pitches data-driven, engineering-style governing and structural reform of the two-party system.
highlights: Supports term limits, ending dark money, and banning congressional insider trading [S1]
Platform pillar to 'modernize government': measure program results rather than spending amounts and eliminate waste [S2]
Would close tax loopholes used by the wealthy, including the 'buy-borrow-die' loophole [S1]
Only challenger with elected experience: appointed to the Monroe school board in 2023, then elected unopposed [S4]
Supports securing the border 'humanely' with immigration reform, and opposes the Trump administration's ICE policies [S2]

taxes.basis: Would close tax loopholes used by the wealthy, including 'buy-borrow-die,' as part of his revenue approach.

spending.basis: Central platform pillars are government efficiency, measuring program results, eliminating waste, and reducing the $39T national debt partly through spending cuts — though he also proposes infrastructure and housing investment.

climate.basis: Would incentivize clean energy, though via relaxed permitting rather than carbon pricing or mandates.

experience.basis: Runs explicitly against career politicians and the two-party system, but holds minor elected office (Monroe school board) — an outsider profile with some governing experience.

reform.basis: Central pitch is structural reform: term limits, ending dark money, banning congressional insider trading, and breaking the two-party system as the sole independent.

immigration.basis: Pairs securing the border 'humanely' with immigration reform and opposition to the Trump administration's ICE policies — elements of both poles.

healthcare.basis: Would reduce costs through standardized medical records and hospital price transparency while preserving individual freedom in healthcare — market fixes within the current system, not single-payer.

tech.basis: Supports data-center construction paired with required power-plant development as a jobs strategy; a tech-industry engineer treating tech growth as opportunity.

defense.basis: Would end U.S. involvement in the Iran war while working with allies to restore global stability — one element from each pole.

candidates.summary: Home care aide from Redmond and first-time candidate running to the incumbent's left, citing five-plus years of caregiving for his brother, who depends on Medicaid, as his reason for running.
highlights: Supports Medicare for All and opposes any further cuts to Medicaid and Medicare [S1]
Calls for wealth taxes on wealth over $1 billion, framed as making 'billionaires illegal' [S2]
Supports national rent stabilization, a $25 federal minimum wage, regulating AI, and halting data center construction [S1]
Would halt all U.S. funding and military aid to Israel and supports abolishing ICE [S4]
Accepts no corporate PAC, AIPAC, or big-tech money [S1]

taxes.basis: Repeatedly calls for a billionaires tax and wealth taxes aimed at taxing 'the billionaire class out of existence,' with rigorous enforcement against the top 1%.

spending.basis: Major public housing investment funded by corporate taxes, a $25 federal minimum wage, and expanded public programs — investment as the primary lever.

climate.basis: Supports clean energy investment and expanded public transit.

experience.basis: First-time candidate, 28, with no public-office record, running on lived caregiving experience explicitly contrasted with the incumbent's tenure and wealth.

reform.basis: Refuses corporate PAC, AIPAC, and big-tech money; calls for ending 'the corporate takeover of our politics' and reforming the Democratic Party.

immigration.basis: Supports abolishing ICE and 'humane immigration that understands these are human people.'

healthcare.basis: Supports Medicare for All / a publicly funded universal system, repeated across pamphlet, website, and press coverage.

social.basis: Would fight to codify Roe, defend gender-affirming care, and protect trans rights and bodily autonomy.

tech.basis: Would regulate AI, halt data center construction, and break up tech monopolies including Apple, Google, Meta, Amazon, and Microsoft.

defense.basis: Calls for halting all U.S. funding and military aid to Israel and refuses AIPAC contributions.

candidates.summary: Clinical audiologist from Everett with 20 years of practice experience and long-time Republican precinct committee officer service; the only Republican-preference candidate in the seven-way field.
highlights: Platform centers on reshoring manufacturing, cybersecurity investment, and tax incentives for small and medium-sized businesses [S1]
Supports controlled immigration with border security and opposes open borders [S3]
Told the Everett Herald she is 'not a big believer in the climate change narrative' [S3]
Endorsed by the King County Republican Party and Stand for Health Freedom [S3]

taxes.basis: Proposes small-business tax incentives and reducing the overall tax burden, while also targeting offshore tax havens and corporate welfare.

climate.basis: Says she is 'not a big believer in the climate change narrative,' expressing support only for land stewardship.

experience.basis: No elected-office record; experience is clinical/managerial (audiology practice) plus party-organizational roles, in a challenge to a fourteen-year incumbent.

immigration.basis: Supports controlled immigration with border security and opposes open borders.

healthcare.basis: Opposes large insurance-healthcare conglomerates and emphasizes individual choice within the existing system; no support for single-payer transformation.

defense.basis: Campaign website questions U.S. support for Israeli military actions.

candidates.summary: Travel agency owner and certified personal trainer from Mountlake Terrace making her first run for office. Describes herself as one of the 'young progressives' running nationwide, with climate, housing, and universal healthcare as top priorities.
highlights: Supports Medicare for All, framing healthcare as 'a human right, not an industry' [S3]
Would increase EPA funding and enforcement, invest in renewables, and expand rail and transit over highways [S3]
Supports term limits in all branches and calls for eliminating lobbying, which she characterizes as 'bribery' [S3]
Would substantially reduce defense spending [S5]
Opposes current immigration enforcement practices, calling them 'barbaric' [S5]

spending.basis: Proposes expanded EPA and Department of Education funding, federal subsidized housing, government-run grocery stores, SNAP expansion, loan forgiveness, and free public college.

climate.basis: Names climate change a top priority: increased EPA funding and enforcement, stricter environmental protections, renewable investment, grid rebuilding, and transit/rail over highways.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected or governmental experience who identifies with the 'young progressives' running nationwide.

reform.basis: Supports term limits in all branches, calls lobbying 'bribery' and would eliminate it, and wants to end multi-million-dollar political campaigns.

immigration.basis: Supports major reform so immigrants can 'come here safely and live, work, and participate,' and calls current enforcement practices 'barbaric.'

healthcare.basis: Supports Medicare for All and a universal healthcare system, opposing profit-driven pharmaceutical and insurance companies.

defense.basis: Would substantially reduce defense spending and opposes what she describes as 'an unauthorized war that has only brought death and disaster.'

contests.office_does: A U.S. Representative writes and votes on federal legislation, controls federal spending through appropriations, conducts oversight of the executive branch, and provides constituent services for the district.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Pramila Jayapal (D), first elected in 2016, faces three first-time federal challengers in one of the safest Democratic seats in the country. Under the top-two primary, the practical question is which challenger advances with her to the November ballot.

candidates.summary: Incumbent U.S. Representative since 2017 and former Washington State Senator; she chairs emerita the Congressional Progressive Caucus and is Ranking Member of the House Immigration Subcommittee.
highlights: Lead sponsor of the Medicare for All Act, framing universal healthcare as a right [S1]
Has sponsored 193 pieces of legislation over nine years in the House, including the Ultra-Millionaire Tax Act and ICE Out of Our Faces Act [S3]
Founded and led the immigrant-advocacy organization OneAmerica for 12 years before elected office [S2]
States she has never taken corporate PAC money and leads bills to ban congressional stock trading [S1]
Endorsed by the Washington State Labor Council, Planned Parenthood, Sierra Club, and King County Democrats, among others [S1]

taxes.basis: Backs an ultra-millionaire wealth tax in her pamphlet statement and sponsored the Ultra-Millionaire Tax Act of 2026 (H.R. 8085).

spending.basis: Platform and sponsored bills center on expanding federal programs: Medicare for All, tuition-free college, and housing guaranteed as a human right.

safety.basis: Sponsored the Housing not Handcuffs Act of 2025 (H.R. 4182), pointing away from criminalization and toward housing/services responses.

climate.basis: States she fights climate change in pamphlet and campaign materials, though the dossier records no specific climate legislation.

experience.basis: Nine years in the House with 193 sponsored bills, committee assignments, and Progressive Caucus leadership; the only candidate with a legislative record.

reform.basis: Says she has never taken corporate PAC money; leads bills to ban congressional stock trading and supports Supreme Court and campaign-finance reform.

immigration.basis: OneAmerica founder who campaigns against ICE/CBP enforcement actions and sponsored the ICE Out of Our Faces Act and Dignity for Detained Immigrants Act.

healthcare.basis: Lead sponsor of the Medicare for All Act, framing universal healthcare as a right, not a privilege.

social.basis: Co-chairs the Trans Equality Task Force and sponsored a resolution supporting a Transgender Bill of Rights.

defense.basis: Authored an Iran War Powers resolution and a bill to sunset the 2001 AUMF, but voted for Iron Dome funding despite a generally pro-Palestinian stance.

candidates.summary: Political and labor organizer and grocery worker who moved to Washington from Missouri in 2023; she is a first-time candidate running under a Democratic Party preference.
highlights: Grocery worker and union strike captain who organized coworkers against corporate grocery mergers [S1]
Supports universal healthcare, free education, and publicly owned utilities and grocery stores [S1]
Runs explicitly against the Democratic establishment, saying her aim is to take over the Democratic Party first [S1]

taxes.basis: Frames her candidacy around fighting billionaires and public ownership, implying but not stating a progressive-revenue position.

spending.basis: Pamphlet platform of universal healthcare, free education for all, and publicly owned utilities and grocery stores is a maximal public-investment program.

experience.basis: No elected or governmental record; runs on labor-organizing experience and explicitly against incumbent leadership.

reform.basis: Says the Democratic Party has zero interest in fighting the billionaires and frames incumbent leadership as failed, though via taking over the party rather than structural reforms.

healthcare.basis: Supports universal healthcare and says she has personally knocked thousands of doors for it.

social.basis: Attributes her move to Washington to escaping anti-transgender discrimination and seeking healthcare access; no explicit policy platform on social issues.

candidates.summary: First-time candidate running under a Republican Party preference; a Marine veteran and former Lakewood police officer who immigrated from India and now runs family businesses in Pierce County.
highlights: Campaigns on bringing law and order back to Seattle and funding law enforcement and emergency services [S1]
Marine veteran (2011-2014) and former Lakewood police officer [S1]
Supports energy independence and investment in electric-grid resilience [S2]
Promotes legal immigration pathways; a news column reports he has posted content opposing birthright citizenship [S4]

spending.basis: Promises military-grade accountability in Washington, D.C. and careful stewardship of tax dollars, while also backing infrastructure and public-safety funding.

safety.basis: Lead campaign theme is bringing law and order back to Seattle, with support for funding law enforcement, drawing on his own policing background.

climate.basis: Cites energy costs as a breaking point for the district and campaigns on energy independence rather than climate policy.

experience.basis: No prior elected office; runs on military, policing, and small-business experience rather than a governmental record.

immigration.basis: Promotes legal-pathway immigration on his website while a news column reports he has posted content opposing birthright citizenship.

candidates.summary: Perennial Seattle-area candidate running under the Fifth Republic Party preference; a former wildlife biologist who has run unsuccessfully for school board and governor and holds no elected office.
highlights: Platform is centered on explicitly antisemitic conspiracy claims in his pamphlet statement and campaign website [S1]
Perennial candidate: school board runs in 1999, 2007, and 2015 and gubernatorial runs in 2016 and 2020, all unsuccessful [S3]
Calls the environment his number-one issue, with an Earth First theme on his website [S2]
Proposes downsizing the military to fund free healthcare and cutting all ties with Israel [S1]

climate.basis: Calls the environment his number-one issue and puts the natural world before corporate profits, though without specific climate or energy legislation proposals.

experience.basis: Has never held elected office across repeated runs since 1999 and submitted no elected or professional experience to the pamphlet.

healthcare.basis: Proposes free healthcare, funded by downsizing the military.

defense.basis: Proposes downsizing the military and cutting all ties with and derecognizing Israel, positions embedded in his broader antisemitic platform.

contests.office_does: A U.S. Representative serves a two-year term in the U.S. House, writing and voting on federal legislation, conducting oversight, directing federal appropriations to the district, and providing constituent services with federal agencies.
contests.race_blurb: Four-term incumbent Democrat Kim Schrier faces four Republicans and a perennial Democratic-preference candidate in this cross-Cascades district's top-two primary. Schrier holds a large fundraising advantage, while the Republican field competes for the second general-election slot with split county-party endorsements.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democrat and pediatrician seeking a fifth term, serving on the House Energy & Commerce Committee. Her campaign centers on healthcare costs, district earmark funding, and a bipartisan governing style.
highlights: Pediatrician for 17 years before Congress; incumbent since 2019, serving on House Energy & Commerce [S1]
Says she passed measures to cap insulin costs and give Medicare drug-price negotiation authority [S1]
Says she secured over $60 million in Community Project (earmark) funding across the district [S1]
Sponsored 104 bills since 2019, including housing, child care, and prescribed-fire legislation [S3]
Endorsed by Planned Parenthood Action Fund, Washington State Labor Council, and League of Conservation Voters [S1]

taxes.basis: Introduced a federal gas-tax suspension while criticizing the Republican majority for pairing tax cuts with healthcare cuts; no explicit position on raising or cutting overall revenue.

spending.basis: Record centers on directing federal investment — $60M+ in district earmarks and sponsored bills expanding housing, child care, and health programs.

climate.basis: Supports spurring clean-energy development and lists environment as a campaign priority; endorsed by League of Conservation Voters.

experience.basis: Four-term incumbent with committee assignments, 104 sponsored bills, and a district earmark record — the maximal proven-record profile in this field.

reform.basis: Runs on bipartisan coalition-building within existing party structures with major institutional endorsements and dominant fundraising; no structural-reform planks in the dossier.

healthcare.basis: Signature issue is strengthening the current system — insulin caps, Medicare price negotiation, insurance regulation — not single-payer replacement.

social.basis: Endorsed by Planned Parenthood Action Fund and lists women's health as a core campaign issue.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate, a Vietnamese-American refugee with a UC Berkeley Ph.D. who spent six years as a federal consultant working on VA fraud prevention. She runs on lowering costs, border security, and government accountability.
highlights: Federal consultant with the VA for six years on fraud prevention; says she helped recover millions in taxpayer funds [S1]
Family fled communist Vietnam; came to the U.S. in 1995 through a humanitarian program [S1]
Runs to lower costs for families, strengthen public safety, and secure the border [S1]
Signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge to support congressional term limits [S6]
Second-best-funded Republican challenger, with roughly $103,000 raised [S4]

spending.basis: Centers her candidacy on rooting out government waste, drawing on her VA fraud-prevention career as insider knowledge of mismanagement.

safety.basis: Wants to strengthen public safety; campaign lists 'Safe Streets' as a priority.

experience.basis: First run for office ('I'm not a career politician') with a renewal theme, though she also pitches federal-agency insider experience.

reform.basis: Signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge to support congressional term limits.

immigration.basis: Calls for securing the border; campaign frames it as 'Smart Immigration.'

social.basis: Campaign centers on faith, family, and personal responsibility; wants schools to 'restore focus on core academics' with parental engagement.

candidates.summary: Republican small business owner and restaurateur in Leavenworth making his first run for office. He runs on lowering the cost of living, supporting small business, and border security, with endorsements from the King County Republican Party.
highlights: Small business owner and restaurateur in Leavenworth; no prior elected office [S1]
Core message is lowering housing, grocery, fuel, and insurance costs [S1]
Endorsed by the King County Republican Party, the only challenger with that endorsement [S1]
Best-funded Republican challenger, with about $126,000 raised [S3]

safety.basis: Lists 'Protecting our communities' / 'Safe Communities' as a priority; direction inferred, no detailed safety platform in the dossier.

experience.basis: Explicit citizen-candidate framing — 'I'm not a politician' — running on private-sector business experience with no prior office.

immigration.basis: Supports keeping the border secure; campaign frames it as 'Smart & Secure Immigration.'

social.basis: Supports parental rights in education; campaign emphasizes his faith.

candidates.summary: Democratic-preference perennial candidate and career federal employee who has run for this seat in every cycle since 2006. His platform centers on ranked-choice voting, with an idiosyncratic mix of progressive economics and a pro-life stance.
highlights: Ranked-choice voting is his central issue, which he calls 'the best democracy defense' [S1]
Supports a progressive tax system on higher incomes, a living wage, and universal healthcare [S1]
Career federal employee (IRS, NOAA) who disputes his termination for running for Congress [S1]
Has run for this seat every cycle since 2006 without advancing past the primary [S3]

taxes.basis: Pamphlet statement supports a progressive tax system on higher incomes.

climate.basis: Supports clean, green, renewable energy sources.

experience.basis: Anti-system outsider with no elected office and a two-decade history of challenging incumbents in this district.

reform.basis: Ranked-choice voting dominates his platform as 'the best democracy defense,' paired with structural theories about limiting judicial power.

immigration.basis: Would focus immigration enforcement on abusive employers rather than on immigrants themselves.

healthcare.basis: Pamphlet statement supports universal healthcare.

social.basis: Describes himself as 'ProLife' with exceptions for rape, incest, and significant health risk.

defense.basis: Opposed starting the Iraq and Afghanistan wars; supports 'UN-focused world unity.'

candidates.summary: Republican data scientist and longtime party officer, including a term as Snohomish County GOP chairman, who has run for county executive and lieutenant governor. He runs on lowering costs, strong policing, and border security paired with agricultural labor protections.
highlights: Four decades in science, engineering, and data science, including healthcare-data work for government and commercial clients [S1]
Former Snohomish County Republican Party chairman and repeat statewide/countywide candidate [S4]
Supports strong policing and opposes lenient prosecutors [S2]
Supports border security while protecting agricultural communities [S2]
Endorsed by the Douglas County Republican Party over the other three Republicans [S5]

taxes.basis: Wants voters to 'keep more of your hard-earned money,' framed around reducing the cost of living.

safety.basis: Supports strong policing and explicitly opposes lenient prosecutors.

climate.basis: Supports clean energy and 'environmental leadership' but conditions it on not 'punishing working families' — a genuinely mixed position.

experience.basis: No elected record and runs on private-sector problem-solving, but his long party-officer tenure and repeat candidacies temper the outsider profile.

immigration.basis: Supports border security while protecting agricultural communities.

social.basis: Supports empowering parents and local communities over bureaucracies in education.

defense.basis: Foreign policy of 'peace through strength' with American interests prioritized.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate with a career in frontline social services and corrections, including work as a parole officer in the juvenile and adult systems. His platform rests on three pillars: justice, accountability, and affordability.
highlights: Parole officer and corrections/social-services career shapes a public-safety-first platform [S1]
Supports federal prosecutions for violent gang and gun crimes [S1]
Wants federal audits, including of Washington state government 'waste, mismanagement, and broken systems' [S1]
Supports permanent tax relief for seniors and working Americans; would eliminate capital gains taxes [S2]
Runs as an underdog with 'no establishment backing, no flashy ads, no big money' [S1]

taxes.basis: Supports permanently securing tax relief and eliminating capital gains taxes, stated in both his pamphlet and campaign site.

spending.basis: Accountability pillar calls for federal audits and investigations of government waste and mismanagement before new spending.

safety.basis: Justice pillar centers on federal prosecution of violent gang and gun crimes, grounded in his corrections and parole-officer career.

experience.basis: Calls himself 'a public servant, not a politician' and pitches generational change; only elected experience is high school class president.

contests.office_does: A U.S. Representative serves a two-year term in the House, writing and voting on federal law, appropriating federal spending, overseeing the executive branch, and providing constituent services with federal agencies.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Adam Smith, the top Democrat on the House Armed Services Committee, faces three challengers running to his left — Kshama Sawant, Melissa Chaudhry, and Jacob Perasso — largely over U.S. support for Israel, war spending, and PAC money, plus Republican Doug Basler. The top two finishers in the August 4 primary advance to the November general election.

candidates.summary: Democratic incumbent representing WA-9 since 1997 and Ranking Member of the House Armed Services Committee. He campaigns on housing costs, workforce development, abortion rights, and a path to single-payer healthcare.
highlights: In Congress since 1997; Ranking Member and past Chairman of the House Armed Services Committee, which drafts the annual defense authorization bill [S1]
Primary sponsor of 7 bills enacted into law, including the FY2021 National Defense Authorization Act and the Promoting Affordable Housing Near Transit Act [S5]
Says the healthcare system should be restructured to create a path to a single-payer plan like Medicare for All [S1]
Raised about $1.13M this cycle, including $420K from PACs and other committees [S4]; past donors include AIPAC and defense contractors [S6]
Endorsed by Gov. Bob Ferguson, more than 20 state legislators, several suburban mayors, and major labor unions [S3]

spending.basis: Sponsored bills concentrate on expanding programs — behavioral health crisis centers, pre-apprenticeship support funds, affordable housing near transit.

climate.basis: Campaign lists protecting Puget Sound as a priority and he is endorsed by the Sierra Club.

experience.basis: 29-year incumbent, HASC Ranking Member, and the only candidate with a federal legislative record including 7 enacted bills.

reform.basis: Accepts PAC money ($420K in committee contributions this cycle) and runs on establishment endorsements and coalition-building within the party.

healthcare.basis: Backs restructuring toward a 'path to a single-payer plan like Medicare for All' — supportive of single-payer as a goal but incremental in mechanism.

social.basis: Lists protecting abortion rights as a campaign priority and is endorsed by Planned Parenthood Action Fund.

defense.basis: NDAA author and HASC Ranking Member who voted for recent defense authorizations and accepts AIPAC and defense-industry donations, though he cites breaks with AIPAC on the Iran deal and UNRWA.

candidates.summary: Democrat and 2024 runner-up in this district, a nonprofit grants and research professional. Her platform centers on ending U.S. funding for Israel's war, abolishing ICE, a billionaire wealth tax, universal healthcare, and structural democracy reforms.
highlights: Ran against Smith in 2024, advancing from the primary and receiving over 90,000 general-election votes [S1]
Platform includes a 35% tax on wealth above $1 billion, a $25/hr minimum wage, a public jobs guarantee, and universal healthcare [S2]
Calls to abolish ICE and close detention centers; represented her husband's 124-day ICE detention case pro se and secured his release [S3]
Says 'Stop funding Israel,' backs auditing the Pentagon, and would restore war powers to Congress [S2]
Takes no corporate PAC money and rejects AIPAC contributions; backs term limits, ranked-choice voting, and overturning Citizens United [S2]

taxes.basis: Proposes a 35% tax on wealth above $1 billion and closing the carried-interest loophole to fund public programs.

spending.basis: Platform of expanded public programs: public jobs guarantee, UBI pilots, universal healthcare, fully funding FEMA, and public banking.

safety.basis: Would end for-profit prisons and ban predictive policing and facial recognition in public spaces.

climate.basis: Calls for renewable energy at scale, fully funding FEMA, and blocking data-center approvals without environmental review and public consent.

experience.basis: No elected-office record; runs on nonprofit, direct-service, and pro se litigation experience as a challenger to a 29-year incumbent.

reform.basis: Refuses corporate PAC and AIPAC money and platforms a stock-trading ban, term limits, court reform, RCV, and overturning Citizens United.

immigration.basis: Would abolish ICE, restructure DHS, close detention centers, and create a path to citizenship; her husband's ICE detention is her signature story.

healthcare.basis: Platform calls for universal healthcare 'guaranteed, comprehensive, as a matter of basic social infrastructure.'

tech.basis: Would ban AI surveillance, facial recognition, and predictive policing; treats data ownership as a Fourth Amendment right; conditions data-center approvals on review.

defense.basis: Says 'Stop funding Israel,' would audit the Pentagon, restore war powers to Congress, reduce the overseas military footprint, and ban weapons-maker donations.

candidates.summary: Former three-term Seattle City Councilmember running as an independent socialist. She campaigns on an arms embargo against Israel, free healthcare funded by taxing the rich, national rent control, and building a new party for working people.
highlights: Served a decade on the Seattle City Council (2014–2024) as its sole socialist; led the movement for the nation-leading $15 minimum wage [S5]
States her first priority in Congress would be an arms embargo against Israel; ends all U.S. military aid to Israel [S1]
Pledges free healthcare for all funded by taxing the rich, national rent control, and a $25/hr minimum wage [S1]
Raised about $609K this cycle, effectively all from individual donations, with no PAC money [S3]
Received The Stranger's sole endorsement in this race [S6]; her council tenure was polarizing, including a survived 2021 recall [S4][S5]

taxes.basis: Campaigns on 'tax the rich' to fund free healthcare and championed Seattle's JumpStart payroll tax on large employers.

spending.basis: Record and platform of expanded public programs: JumpStart-funded affordable housing, publicly funded eviction defense, millions of affordable homes.

experience.basis: A decade of enacted municipal legislation (proven record) combined with an explicitly outsider campaign against both parties to build a new workers' party.

reform.basis: Refuses corporate money (all $609K from individuals), pledges to take only an average worker's wage, and runs to build a new party outside the two-party system.

immigration.basis: Pledges to 'shut down ICE and the detention centers.'

healthcare.basis: Pledges 'free healthcare for all funded by taxing the rich' — the only candidate in the race making this specific pledge.

social.basis: Championed Seattle's first-in-the-nation ban on caste-based discrimination and Indigenous Peoples' Day.

tech.basis: Campaign plank to 'Stop the Data Centers & AI Layoffs' through democratic public ownership.

defense.basis: Ending all U.S. military aid to Israel is her stated first priority (an arms embargo), in an explicitly antiwar campaign against the HASC Ranking Member.

candidates.summary: Republican advertising and video production business owner and former King County Republican Party vice chairman, making his sixth run against Adam Smith. He campaigns on cost of living, energy affordability, and public safety.
highlights: Says the state's Climate Commitment Act is driving up energy costs 'while doing little to directly improve the environment' and cites high gas taxes as raising costs [S1]
Argues 'big government policies are making it harder to build, hire, afford, and stay' and frames his campaign as an 'opportunity revolution' [S1]
On public safety: compassion for people in crisis 'while still holding people accountable for crime, addiction-fueled disorder' [S2]
Repeat challenger to Smith with general-election results of 25.7% (2020) and 28.2% (2022) [S4]; reported $0 in 2026 fundraising receipts [S3]

taxes.basis: Cites high gas taxes as raising transportation, food, and energy costs and blames 'big government policies' for affordability problems.

spending.basis: Argues big-government policies and regulation are making it harder to build, hire, and afford — a restraint-first framing.

safety.basis: Pairs compassion for people in crisis with 'holding people accountable for crime, addiction-fueled disorder' — an accountability-leaning stance.

climate.basis: Says the Climate Commitment Act raises energy costs while doing little for the environment — the race's only energy-affordability-first position.

experience.basis: No elected-office record, running as a small-business owner, though a longtime local party officer and repeat candidate.

candidates.summary: Seattle bakery worker, union member, and Socialist Workers Party candidate. He campaigns on ending U.S. wars in the Middle East, a federally funded public works program at union wages, amnesty for immigrants, and building a revolutionary workers' party.
highlights: Calls for 'US Out of the Middle East,' opposes the U.S. war against Iran, and calls for U.S. troops and nuclear weapons out of Europe, while defending Ukraine's sovereignty and Israel's right to exist as a refuge for Jews [S1]
Backs a federally funded public works program at union wages, a shorter workweek with no loss in pay, and amnesty for immigrants [S1]
SWP member since 2000 who argues billionaire families rule through both major parties and that workers need a revolutionary political party [S1]
No elected-office record and no FEC campaign committee registered for the 2026 cycle [S4]

spending.basis: Calls for a federally funded public works program at union wages and a shorter workweek with no loss in pay.

safety.basis: Long history of organizing protests against police brutality — an indirect prevention-and-rights lean; no current enforcement platform.

experience.basis: No elected-office record; a working-trade movement candidate whose campaign is oriented to party-building and rallies rather than legislation.

reform.basis: Argues billionaires rule through both major parties, calls for a new revolutionary workers' party, and runs with no registered fundraising committee.

immigration.basis: Calls for amnesty for immigrants; began his activism organizing for immigrant rights.

defense.basis: Calls for U.S. out of the Middle East and U.S. troops and nuclear weapons out of Europe, though unlike other left candidates he defends Israel's right to exist rather than centering an aid cutoff.

contests.office_does: A King County Councilmember helps adopt the county's roughly $20 billion biennial budget and sets policy for Metro Transit, public health, the jail and courts, behavioral health, homelessness response, elections, and unincorporated communities.
contests.race_blurb: An open seat after Girmay Zahilay became County Executive, contested mainly between two sitting progressives — State Sen. Rebecca Saldaña and Port Commissioner Toshiko Hasegawa — with first-time candidate and county employee Miriam Mboya also running. The sharpest split is how to fund social housing.

candidates.summary: A King County employee of seven years in budgeting, operations, and contracting, and a first-time candidate from Skyway running on lived experience with homelessness, foster care, and immigration.
highlights: Would 'massively expand investment in affordable housing' and protect renters from exploitative practices [S2]
Prevention over enforcement: deeper investment in mental health and addiction services, expanded diversion, and restorative pathways instead of incarceration [S2]
Runs on internal county operations knowledge from seven years as a county employee, plus lived experience with homelessness and the foster system [S1]
Has not articulated specific revenue positions; raised about $44K, roughly a fifth of each opponent [S3]

spending.basis: Platform leads with expanded investment in housing, behavioral health, and job programs, paired with budget-stewardship language.

housing.basis: Calls for massively expanded affordable-housing investment and renter protections; no market-supply planks.

safety.basis: Explicit prevention-over-enforcement platform: diversion expansion and restorative pathways rather than incarceration.

experience.basis: The only candidate without elected office, running on lived experience and a county-staff perspective against two sitting officeholders.

candidates.summary: A Port of Seattle Commissioner (president in 2025) and former state agency director, backing a county social-housing levy now and running on executive project delivery; endorsed by both former governors and Executive Zahilay.
highlights: Backs a county levy for social housing now — 'I believe that we need to act with urgency' — the race's sharpest policy split [S4]
Has proposed lidding I-5 for over 1,000 affordable units, dedicating county land near transit to community land trusts, and a public bank for housing [S3]
Would prioritize maintaining bus service over fleet electrification if forced to choose, and wants to accelerate the RapidRide R Line [S4]
Says she will champion progressive funding options 'so the ultra-wealthy pay their share' and fund mental health and community-led gun-violence prevention [S1]
Executive résumé: six years leading a state agency under Gov. Inslee, Port commission presidency, and prior police-accountability work at OLEO [S1]

taxes.basis: Champions progressive funding so the ultra-wealthy pay their share and supports a new county social-housing levy now.

spending.basis: Urgency-framed investment agenda — social-housing levy, mental-health funding, childcare expansion — delivered through an executive lens.

housing.basis: Public-tools platform: county social-housing levy, community land trusts on county land, a public bank, and the I-5 lid housing proposal.

safety.basis: Would fund mental-health systems and community-led gun-violence prevention; background in police-accountability oversight at OLEO.

experience.basis: Sitting elected official with an executive record — Port commissioner since 2022 and six years as a cabinet-level agency director — though a shorter elected tenure than her main rival.

candidates.summary: The 37th District state senator since 2016 and Senate Labor & Commerce chair, with a decade-long legislative record, holding the King County Democrats' sole endorsement and the service-worker union lane.
highlights: Prime-sponsored and passed the HEAL Act, the Washington Voting Rights Act, and the Domestic Workers Bill of Rights [S3]
Campaigns on taxing the ultra-wealthy and large corporations; says she championed the capital gains tax (her own site describes her as a co-sponsor) and a 'Millionaires Tax' in the senate [S1][S2]
Reticent about a county property-tax levy for social housing, preferring progressive revenue options and state-level action [S4]
Wants transit 'fast, reliable, and free' and supports a countywide transit levy; backs universal childcare and a $25 minimum wage push [S2]
Holds the King County Democrats' sole endorsement, MLK Labor, and major service-worker and county-employee unions [S1]

taxes.basis: Record and platform of taxing the ultra-wealthy — capital gains and millionaires-tax advocacy — while preferring progressive tools over property-tax levies.

spending.basis: Investment-forward platform: universal childcare, expanded behavioral health, free-transit ambitions, and wage supports.

housing.basis: Public-investment approach to housing 'affordable at any income,' though hesitant on the county social-housing levy in favor of state-level tools.

safety.basis: Pledges to expand behavioral health and crisis response; no enforcement-side planks in the dossier.

climate.basis: Prime-sponsored the HEAL Act, the first statewide environmental-justice law, and claims contributions to the Climate Commitment Act and Clean Fuel Standard.

experience.basis: A decade in the state senate with a committee chairmanship and multiple prime-sponsored laws — the deepest legislative record in the race.

contests.office_does: A Seattle City Council district member legislates for the city: passing ordinances, adopting the budget, setting land-use and tax policy, and overseeing departments including police, transportation, and housing.
contests.race_blurb: A special election to fill the District 5 (North Seattle) seat vacated by Cathy Moore, currently held on an interim basis by appointee Debora Juarez. None of the four candidates has held elected office: FairVote organizer Nilu Jenks holds the institutional-progressive endorsement bloc, educator-banker Julie Kang has the Seattle Times and centrist-official backing, democratic socialist Silas James runs the field's left flank, and broker Dimitri Georgakopoulos runs an enforcement-and-business campaign with minimal resources.

candidates.summary: FairVote Washington political director, Lake City parent, and first-generation Iranian American making her second run for this seat after finishing third in the 2023 primary. She holds the race's broadest institutional-progressive endorsement base and its largest fundraising total.
highlights: Champions 1,000 new affordable housing units in the district plus just-cause eviction, rent stabilization, and a tenant right to counsel [S4][S1]
Opposes mandatory treatment conditions for shelter; supports housing-first with voluntary treatment and community responder programs, and opposes surveillance expansion [S4][S3]
Professional focus on democracy reform: works at FairVote Washington and prioritizes successful ranked-choice-voting implementation in 2027 [S1][S3]
Climate advocate supporting increased density, protected bike lanes, and transit connections to light rail [S3][S4]
Endorsed by the King County Democrats, three LD Democratic organizations, Working Families Party, multiple unions, and Councilmember Rinck; leads fundraising at ~$109,000 [S1][S2][S5]

spending.basis: Platform is built on expanding public investment — affordable-housing targets, treatment and responder programs, free student transit — without an audit-first framing.

housing.basis: Backs rent stabilization, just-cause eviction, tenant right to counsel, and a 1,000-unit affordable-housing target — the public-investment-and-tenant-protection pole — alongside general density support.

safety.basis: Housing-first with voluntary treatment, community responder programs, opposition to treatment-conditioned shelter, and opposition to surveillance expansion.

climate.basis: Pledges to move the city toward meeting its climate goals and ties her density and transit agenda to them; press describes her as a climate advocate.

experience.basis: Has never held elected office — an organizer/advocate profile — though a repeat candidate and 2025 appointment finalist with sustained district engagement.

reform.basis: Structural political reform is her professional record and a stated priority: FairVote Washington political director committed to implementing ranked choice voting in 2027.

candidates.summary: Seattle University educator, 30-year District 5 resident, and co-founder of a Korean-American community bank, endorsed by the Seattle Times and a coalition spanning centrist councilmembers and Democratic legislators. She pairs rebuilding police staffing with expanded treatment and a permitting-reform housing agenda.
