highlights: Top priority pairs rebuilding SPD staffing and a second District 5 precinct with shutting down open-air drug markets and expanding on-demand treatment [S2]
Housing platform centers permitting reform, middle housing, and transit-oriented development, with affordable-housing funding shifted toward payroll-tax revenue [S1][S2]
Suggests combining budget cuts with selective new taxes and describes her decision-making as relying on "data, audits, and lived experience" [S3][S2]
Says she will maintain Seattle's sanctuary-city status and protect immigrant workers and businesses [S1][S2]
Endorsed by the Seattle Times editorial board, the 32nd LD Democrats, and councilmembers from the body's centrist wing; ~$107,000 raised [S5][S1][S2][S6]

taxes.basis: Mixed posture: would combine budget cuts with selective new taxes, and would shift affordable-housing funding onto existing payroll-tax revenue rather than proposing new progressive revenue.

spending.basis: Frames decisions around data and audits, proposes budget cuts alongside selective taxes, and stresses transparent reporting and measurable improvements across city systems.

housing.basis: Leads with market-supply tools — shorter permit review, middle housing, TOD, infrastructure concurrency — while also backing renter displacement protections and public affordable-housing funding.

safety.basis: The field's clearest supporter of rebuilding SPD staffing, a second precinct, and closing open-air drug markets, paired with treatment expansion and behavioral-health investment; sources conflict on SOAP zones.

experience.basis: Has never held elected office; runs on a professional educator-and-community-banker background rather than a governmental record.

candidates.summary: Disabled educator, researcher, and former Seattle Disability Commission chair running as a self-described democratic socialist. His platform — corporate taxes, guaranteed basic income, social housing at 30-40% of stock, and opposition to surveillance contracts — is the furthest left in the race.
highlights: Proposes taxing wealthy corporations, a vacancy tax on residential and commercial property, and a $500/month guaranteed basic income for lower-income households [S1][S2][S4]
Advocates social housing reaching 30-40% of Seattle's housing stock, tenant unions with enforceable penalties, and opposes encampment sweeps [S4][S3]
Frames poverty as the primary public-safety threat; supports restorative justice for nonviolent offenses and CARE-team expansion with independent dispatch authority [S4][S3]
Opposes license-plate readers, CCTV, and the city's Axon contracts, citing cost and ICE connections [S2][S3][S4]
Centers disability access and universal design; raised ~$45,000 with no organizational endorsements found [S1][S3][S5]

taxes.basis: Repeated, platform-central proposals to tax wealthy corporations and vacant properties to fund public services.

spending.basis: Expansion of public programs is his primary lever: guaranteed basic income (submitted to the Council and Mayor), social housing, and corporate-tax-funded community resilience systems.

housing.basis: Social housing at 30-40% of stock, vacancy tax, tenant unions with enforceable penalties, tenants as a protected class, and opposition to sweeps.

safety.basis: Roots public safety in poverty reduction rather than police presence: restorative justice over incarceration, independently dispatched CARE responders, housing-first, and opposition to surveillance tools.

climate.basis: Supports free public transit, dedicated bus and protected bike lanes, and green-jobs development, though climate is not a lead plank.

experience.basis: Self-described democratic socialist outsider with no elected office and no organizational endorsements, running on lived experience navigating benefit systems and advocacy work.

candidates.summary: Longtime Seattle restaurateur turned Windermere real estate broker running an enforcement-and-business-revitalization campaign as a self-described non-politician. He has minimal fundraising, no endorsements found, and a platform thin on specifics beyond public safety and small-business support.
highlights: Says he will work with the council, mayor, and SPD to keep streets "free of Drug abusers, homeless encampments and violent offenders" [S2]
Focuses on support for District 5 businesses and criticizes bike lanes multiplying "while our commercial corridors go quiet" [S1]
Skeptical of familiar campaign promises including "Tax the rich"; emphasizes accountability from institutions and private-sector partnership [S1]
Nearly three decades as a restaurant owner and 13 years as a real estate broker; describes himself as "not a career politician" [S1]
Minimal campaign infrastructure: $2,050 raised, no endorsements found, not yet qualified for democracy vouchers [S3][S4][S2]

taxes.basis: Explicit skepticism of "Tax the rich" campaign promises, though no detailed tax platform is published.

spending.basis: Emphasizes "accountability from our institutions" and private-sector partnership over program expansion; critiques existing city policies rather than proposing new spending.

safety.basis: The race's most enforcement-oriented platform: keeping streets free of drug users, encampments, and violent offenders, framed around business revitalization rather than services.

experience.basis: Small-business owner who has never held office, runs explicitly as "not a career politician," and operates outside the race's endorsement and fundraising infrastructure.

contests.office_does: A King County Councilmember adopts the county's biennial budget and sets policy for public health, behavioral health, Metro Transit, the Sheriff's Office and courts, homelessness and housing investments, and unincorporated-area land use.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent labor-progressive Teresa Mosqueda defends her seat after an even-year election change, challenged by first-time candidate Nick Duda, who runs on fiscal oversight and a firmer line on street disorder and won the Seattle Times endorsement, and by direct-democracy candidate Mia Jacobson.

candidates.summary: A longshoreman and repeat candidate running a process-focused direct-democracy platform — parallel public forums and interactive pin-drop maps — without positions on the county's substantive policy areas.
highlights: Argues 'our local legislative process has no place for public opinion' and pledges an open public forum parallel to every committee [S1]
Proposes an interactive map where residents drop pins to identify issues that guide legislation [S2]
No governmental experience, endorsements, or detected fundraising; single-digit finishes in 2023 and 2025 Seattle primaries [S4]

experience.basis: The only candidate with no governmental experience of any kind, running explicitly anti-establishment against an incumbent and a City Hall aide.

reform.basis: Entire platform is structural reform of how decisions are made: direct-democracy forums and participation tools as a standing check on representatives.

candidates.summary: The incumbent since 2024 with seven prior years on the Seattle City Council, chairing the county Board of Health and consolidating Democratic, labor, and advocacy endorsements behind a services-first, progressive-revenue record.
highlights: Passed county legislation banning rent price-fixing algorithms (2025) and labor-standards procurement rules [S4]; led Seattle's JumpStart payroll tax generating $200M+ annually [S2][S7]

Frames community safety around behavioral-health investment and gun-violence prevention; on Aurora Avenue trafficking, emphasized connecting victims to services — an answer the Seattle Times called nonresponsive [S6]
Housing priorities are expanding affordable and supportive housing and preventing displacement, plus impact-fee legislation to reduce development barriers [S1]
Chairs the Board of Health and sits on the Sound Transit Board; co-sponsored Strategic Climate Action Plan updates [S4]
Endorsed by Gov. Ferguson, AG Brown, Rep. Jayapal, MLK Labor, and 200+ more [S1]; raised roughly ten times her nearest opponent [S5]

taxes.basis: Touts passing Seattle's JumpStart payroll tax as her model and prefers new progressive revenue over cuts.

spending.basis: Expanding investment — housing, behavioral health, childcare, transit — is her stated lever, including a budget amendment backfilling federal cuts.

housing.basis: Record of tenant-side and public-investment action: rent-algorithm ban, doubled affordable-housing investment in Seattle, supportive-housing and anti-displacement priorities.

safety.basis: Approaches safety through behavioral-health investment, gun-violence prevention, and victim services; no enforcement-side planks documented.

climate.basis: Co-sponsored county Strategic Climate Action Plan updates, platforms 'green housing development,' and is endorsed by Washington Conservation Action.

experience.basis: A decade of elected legislative record across two councils, with specific passed legislation and regional board leadership.

reform.basis: Pole_a is 'coalition-building within existing party/donor structures gets results.' The dossier documents exactly that profile: 200+ consolidated institutional Democratic, labor, and advocacy endorsements [S1][S7] and a fundraising operation roughly ten times her nearest opponent's [S5], with no reform planks (PAC refusal, term limits, direct democracy) anywhere in her materials. Strong endorsement pattern supports medium per the guide's confidence tiers.

candidates.summary: A first-time candidate with 17 years in the private sector and 10 months as a Seattle council aide, running on fiscal oversight, measurable outcomes, and firmer enforcement of public drug use; endorsed by the Seattle Times.
highlights: Core message: county government should return to 'accountability, affordability, public safety, and effective services,' with independent auditing and outcome measurement [S1]
On homelessness, says 'compassion without accountability is not a strategy'; wants performance standards for regional homelessness spending and oversight of KCRHA and DCHS [S4]
Favors more aggressive enforcement of public drug use, including a 'three strikes' approach leading to mandatory drug treatment [S4]
Says government frustrates 'willing taxpayers by asking for more and delivering too little'; cites Detroit's decline as his fiscal cautionary tale [S2]
Holds the race's one counter-establishment institutional endorsement, the Seattle Times editorial board; raised about $13K [S4]

taxes.basis: Skeptical of asking taxpayers for more without measurable results, while stopping short of an anti-tax platform ('willing taxpayers').

spending.basis: Oversight is his core message: independent auditing, outcome measurement, and financial oversight of homelessness agencies before expansion.

safety.basis: Backs more aggressive law enforcement on public drug use and a 'three strikes' path to mandatory treatment, while naming treatment and recovery as goals.

experience.basis: Political newcomer offering a fresh private-sector and staff-level approach against a long-tenured incumbent.

contests.office_does: A King County District Court judge in the Northeast Electoral District handles the county's limited-jurisdiction docket — misdemeanor criminal calendars, traffic and other infractions, civil protection orders, and small-claims cases — from the Redmond-area courthouse.
contests.race_blurb: An open seat following Judge Marcus Nailor's retirement has drawn three candidates who all already sit pro tem in this court, so the choice turns on professional formation: Jan Trasen's 26 years in public defense and appellate work plus full-time judging as an ALJ, Josh Schaer's Assistant Attorney General career with pro tem service since 2008, and Bianca Tse's 16 years as a King County prosecutor. All three self-report top bar ratings.

candidates.summary: Administrative Law Judge with the state Office of Administrative Hearings and King County District Court pro tem, after 26 years as a public defender and appellate attorney in Washington and New York. She holds the King County Democrats endorsement and campaigns on treatment-focused approaches to reduce recidivism.
highlights: The only candidate whose day job is full-time judging (ALJ, Child & Family Care Division), alongside pro tem service in this court [S1][S2]
26 years as a public defender and appellate attorney, arguing criminal and dependency cases before the Washington Supreme Court and Court of Appeals [S1]
Advocates mental-health and substance-use treatment programs designed to lower recidivism and strengthen neighborhood safety [S2]
Endorsed by the King County Democrats (independently confirmed) and, per her statement, a majority of the Washington Supreme Court and 75+ judges [S3][S1]
Self-reports "Exceptionally Well Qualified" ratings from five bar associations (unverified) [S1]

judicial.basis: The only candidate campaigning on programmatic change — therapeutic, community-focused court approaches — rather than solely on readiness and fairness.

safety.basis: 26-year defense/appellate formation combined with an explicit platform of mental-health and substance-use treatment programs to reduce recidivism.

experience.basis: Runs on a proven-record case: full-time judicial work as an ALJ, pro tem service in this court, and the largest claimed slate of judicial endorsers.

candidates.summary: Assistant Attorney General with 25 years of courtroom experience spanning public defense, civil practice, and appellate advising, and a King County judge pro tem since 2008. He is the only candidate with prior elected office, having served eight years on the Issaquah City Council.
highlights: Longest part-time judicial service in the race: judge pro tem in King County courts since 2008 [S1]
Career lane is government civil/appellate practice at the Attorney General's Office, with earlier public defense [S1][S2]
Only candidate with prior elected office — eight years on the Issaquah City Council [S2]

Self-reports "Exceptionally Well-Qualified" ratings from four bar associations including KCBA (unverified); endorsed by the 1st LD Democrats, AG Nick Brown, and Executive Zahilay per his statement [S1]

experience.basis: Proven-record profile: 18 years of pro tem judicial service, 25 years of courtroom practice, and eight years in elected office.

candidates.summary: Bellevue attorney and King County District Court judge pro tem who spent 16 years as a King County deputy prosecutor trying cases up to homicide, then built a bilingual private practice serving the Asian community. She leads the field in fundraising and her endorsement list leans toward prosecutors, Eastside officials, and sitting judges.
highlights: 16 years as a King County deputy prosecuting attorney, trying homicides, assaults, robberies, and domestic-violence cases [S1][S3]
Judge pro tem since 2021 in King County District Court itself plus the Mercer Island, Kirkland, and Issaquah municipal courts [S1][S2]
Only candidate whose top bar rating has partial third-party corroboration: the Asian Bar Association of Washington's highest designation, per press coverage [S3]
Endorsed per her pamphlet by former Prosecutor Dan Satterberg, Senator Manka Dhingra, and Eastside officials; leads the race in fundraising (~$42,000) [S1][S4]

safety.basis: Professional formation is 16 years of career prosecution including homicide trials, the only prosecution-side formation in the race; no explicit bail or diversion positions documented.

experience.basis: Runs on a readiness-and-record case — long trial career, five years sitting pro tem in this court, sitting-judge encouragement, and the field's largest war chest.

contests.office_does: A Seattle Municipal Court judge handles the city's limited-jurisdiction docket — misdemeanors and gross misdemeanors, traffic and parking infractions, and specialty calendars like Veterans Treatment Court — and decides bail, release, and diversion for many residents' only contact with the justice system.
contests.race_blurb: Effectively an open seat with retiring Judge Willie Gregory endorsing Garmon Newsom to succeed him. Lindsay Calkins has withdrawn, leaving a two-way contest with a clean professional split: Newsom's 30-year prosecution and municipal-government career (backed by the Seattle Times and the incumbent) versus Gabe Rothstein's 20-year public-defense career (backed by the King County Democrats and public-defense labor).

candidates.summary: Seattle Municipal Court pro tem judge and former Burien City Attorney with a 30-plus-year career as a felony and misdemeanor prosecutor, municipal attorney, and civil litigator. He is endorsed by the retiring incumbent and the Seattle Times editorial board and leads the race in fundraising.
highlights: 30+ years in criminal and civil law: Bronx DA and NYC Special Narcotics prosecutor, Renton Chief Prosecutor, Burien City Attorney, and 75+ trials [S1][S2]
Advocates restorative justice and diversion programs that are "evidence-based, effective, and accountable," with courts working with community partners [S1]
Pro tem judicial service at Seattle Municipal Court itself; self-reports "Exceptionally Well-Qualified" ratings from two bar associations (unverified) [S1]
Endorsed by retiring Position 5 incumbent Judge Willie Gregory and the Seattle Times editorial board [S1][S3]
Leads the race in fundraising at roughly $77,000, more than double his opponent [S4]

judicial.basis: Campaigns on restorative justice, community partnership, and access to justice for marginalized communities — a pragmatic, community-responsive framing, qualified by his accountability emphasis.

safety.basis: Genuinely mixed: career professional formation is prosecution and municipal-government law, while his stated platform centers diversion and restorative justice with an evidence-and-accountability caveat; no explicit bail position documented.

experience.basis: The proven-record candidate: three decades of courtroom and public-sector legal leadership, pro tem service in this court, and the retiring incumbent's succession endorsement.

candidates.summary: Public defender since 2005 with more than 70 trials to verdict, chair of the WSBA Character and Fitness Board, and judge pro tem since 2024 in six regional courts. He is endorsed by the King County Democrats and the SEIU 925 public defense chapter and is the race's most explicit candidate on bail policy.
highlights: 20-year public-defense career including drug-treatment-court attorney, juvenile and civil-commitment unit lead, and 70+ trials to verdict [S1]
States release decisions should be based on flight risk and public safety rather than ability to afford bail [S2]
Prioritizes treatment over punishment in substance-abuse and mental-health cases while balancing victim safety with accountability; serves on the board of an inpatient recovery center [S1][S2]
Judge pro tem since 2024 across six courts, including criminal, jail, and vehicle-impound calendars [S2]
Endorsed by the King County Democrats (independently confirmed), two LD Democratic organizations, and the SEIU 925 Public Defense Chapter [S3][S1]

judicial.basis: Calls Seattle Municipal Court "the people's court" and centers second chances, treatment, and dignity — a community-responsive, reform-open posture on the court's docket.

safety.basis: The race's most explicit prevention-side record: release based on risk rather than bail affordability, treatment over punishment, drug-court and recovery-board service, and a 20-year public-defense formation; medium because he pairs this with victim-safety and accountability language.

experience.basis: Mixed profile: two decades of practice and bar-governance leadership, but judicial service only since 2024 and running against the incumbent-endorsed, editorial-board-backed candidate.

candidates.summary: Trial and appellate attorney with nearly 15 years of litigation experience focused on criminal appeals and resentencing, and the only candidate in the field without pro tem judicial service. She has withdrawn from the race for family reasons, though her statement still appears in the printed pamphlet and her name may remain on the ballot.
highlights: Announced her withdrawal from the race on her campaign website, citing a decision to focus on family [S2]
Nearly 15 years of trial and appellate litigation focused on criminal appeals and resentencing work [S1]
Emphasized access to justice, language access, and courts grounded in the real lives of the people before them [S1]

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 1st Legislative District (Bothell area / north King-Snohomish suburbs), serving a two-year term and voting on state budgets and legislation.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Davina Duerr, who chairs the House Local Government Committee, faces Republican first-time candidate and small-business owner Maggie Wang. With only two candidates, both advance to the November general election regardless of primary results.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state representative first appointed in 2019, chair of the House Local Government Committee and a licensed architect with a prior Bothell City Council career.
highlights: States seven of her bills became law last term, including permit streamlining and lowering the cost of building affordable housing [S1]
Cites securing $532 million for the district in 2025, including $464.5 million for the I-405 corridor project [S1]
Endorsed by the Washington State Labor Council, Washington Conservation Action, and Sierra Club Washington [S1][S2]

experience.basis: Incumbent since 2019, House Local Government Committee chair, seven bills signed last term, prior Bothell council/deputy-mayor service.

spending.basis: Runs on public investment: district transportation funding ($532M secured), affordable-housing subsidy, and education funding priorities.

housing.basis: Record spans both poles: permit-streamlining and cheaper/faster building alongside bills lowering the cost of subsidized affordable housing.

climate.basis: Passed bills phasing out super-pollutant refrigerants and strengthening landfill enforcement; endorsed by Sierra Club and Washington Conservation Action.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate, an immigrant from Taiwan who ran a hair salon and a downtown Redmond restaurant for twenty-six years; volunteer election observer at King County Elections since 2022.
highlights: Opposes tax increases; campaign messaging includes "Stop Tax Increases" and a focus on lower taxes [S1][S2]
Names parental rights and safer neighborhoods as core priorities [S1][S2]
Frames rising taxes, housing costs, and "government overreach" as the main threats to affordability [S1]

taxes.basis: Repeated explicit opposition to tax increases across pamphlet and campaign site; "lower taxes" is her stated focus.

spending.basis: Runs on reduced government spending and frames "government overreach" as a threat to affordability.

social.basis: Names parental rights as a core priority in both pamphlet and campaign materials, without further policy detail.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected experience, running on a small-business outsider profile against a sitting committee chair.

contests.office_does: A State Representative for Legislative District 11 writes and votes on state law, adopts the state operating, capital, and transportation budgets, and sets statewide policy on housing, education, healthcare, taxes, public safety, and transportation.
contests.race_blurb: Two-term-plus Democratic incumbent David Hackney faces an intra-party challenge from nurse anesthetist Ashley Fedan and Republican ironworker Christian Rombough in this Tukwila-Renton-Kent district. The Democrats' stated policy goals overlap heavily, making the contest largely one of profiles — legislative record versus frontline healthcare perspective — with Rombough offering the only cross-party contrast.

candidates.summary: Incumbent State Representative serving since 2021, an attorney and former federal and UN war crimes prosecutor who chairs the Washington State Legislative Black Caucus. He runs on his legislative record and a cost-of-living agenda.
highlights: Incumbent since 2021; Vice Chair of the House Consumer Protection & Business Committee and chair of the Legislative Black Caucus [S3]
States his singular focus is lowering costs for everyday people, from housing to healthcare [S1]
Prime sponsor of HB 1149 (2025) targeting organized animal fighting, signed into law after near-unanimous passage [S5]
Has prioritized clean energy initiatives and environmental justice for his constituents [S4]
Endorsed by the Seattle Times [S8] and Washington State Labor Council [S10], with Governor Ferguson and AG Nick Brown per his pamphlet [S1]; dual-endorsed by King County Democrats [S9]

experience.basis: Five years in the seat with a committee vice-chairmanship, Black Caucus leadership, and enacted legislation — the maximal proven-record profile in this race.

climate.basis: Has prioritized clean energy initiatives and made environmental justice and air quality a top priority per legislative record and the Seattle Times endorsement.

spending.basis: Genuinely mixed: campaigns on bold state-led investment to drive down costs, while listing improving state government performance and protecting taxpayers among 2026 session priorities.

candidates.summary: Nurse anesthetist, small-business owner, Navy veteran, and Democratic Party activist mounting an intra-party challenge to the incumbent. She centers healthcare access and argues the legislature needs a nurse's frontline perspective.
highlights: Registered nurse for over 25 years and CRNA; owns a practice providing anesthesia care, including gender-affirming, abortion, and reproductive care [S1]
Argues no nurses currently serve in the legislature and a nurse's problem-solving perspective is needed in Olympia [S1]
Campaign priorities are cost of living, healthcare access, and defending human dignity against federal government actions [S2]
Dual-endorsed by King County Democrats alongside the incumbent [S5]; her campaign site also lists The Stranger, Stonewall Democrats, and nursing organizations [S2]
Larger small-donor base than the incumbent by contribution count (177 vs. 75 records) at roughly 40% of his dollar total [S3]

experience.basis: Runs explicitly on a frontline-practitioner fresh perspective against a five-year incumbent, though she holds party roles (PCO, DNC delegate, former LD chair) rather than a pure outsider profile.

social.basis: Professionally provides gender-affirming, abortion, and reproductive care; Pro-Choice Washington PAC member with Stonewall Democrats and Planned Parenthood endorsement claims.

spending.basis: Supports affordable housing, K-12 investments, and career training as levers for working families squeezed by costs — an invest-and-expand orientation.

candidates.summary: Army veteran and journeyman union ironworker running as the only Republican in the race. His pitch is anti-Olympia accountability, local control, and public safety, paired with a pledge to defend trade unions.
highlights: Argues Olympia has grown too wasteful and disconnected; pledges to cut red tape, demand accountability, restore local control, and back public safety [S1]
Union ironworker (Ironworkers Local 86) who says he will defend trade unions, an unusual pairing with his Republican party preference [S1]
Signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge supporting an Article V convention resolution for congressional term limits [S3]

spending.basis: Explicit pamphlet statement that Olympia is 'too wasteful, too arrogant'; pledges to cut red tape and demand accountability before anything else.

local-control.basis: Explicitly campaigns on restoring local control as a core plank of his pamphlet statement.

safety.basis: Pledges to back public safety within an anti-Olympia accountability platform; enforcement-leaning framing but no specific policy detail.

reform.basis: Signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge committing to support an Article V convention resolution for congressional term limits.

experience.basis: No elected experience listed; runs a full outsider case against a 'disconnected' Olympia from a tradesman-veteran profile.

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 12th Legislative District (all of Chelan County plus parts of Snohomish and King counties after 2024 redistricting), serving a two-year term in the Washington State House.
contests.race_blurb: First-term Republican incumbent Brian Burnett, a former three-term Chelan County Sheriff, faces Democrat Stacy Willoughby, a Chelan bank branch manager and anti-poverty nonprofit board president. With only two candidates, both advance to the November general election.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Republican state representative first elected in 2024, previously elected Chelan County Sheriff for three terms after more than 25 years in law enforcement.
highlights: Past president of the Washington Association of Sheriffs & Police Chiefs; platform leads with reducing crime and "victim-centered justice that holds criminals accountable" [S1][S2]
Names parental rights and returning decision-making to local communities as priorities [S1]
No endorsements listed in his pamphlet statement or retrieved campaign-site content [S1][S3]

safety.basis: 25+ year law-enforcement career, three terms as elected sheriff, and a platform of crime reduction and holding criminals accountable.

social.basis: Names protecting "your role as a parent" as a core plank, without further policy detail.

spending.basis: Fiscal plank is prioritizing taxpayer dollars and reducing burdens on small businesses — a restraint framing.

local-control.basis: Explicit plank on returning decision-making to local communities.

experience.basis: Twelve years as an elected sheriff plus one legislative term — a proven-elected-record profile, though with a short legislative tenure and no bills cited.

candidates.summary: Democratic first-time candidate from Chelan with 25 years in financial services, currently a bank branch manager and board president of the rent-assistance nonprofit Chelan Valley Hope.
highlights: Would champion a state budget prioritizing "tax fairness for rural families and small businesses" [S1][S2]
Emphasizes affordable housing that serves each community and trades training for youth [S1]
Endorsed by the 12th LD Democrats, King County Democrats, Washington State Labor Council, and AFT-Washington [S1]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record, running on financial-services and nonprofit leadership against an incumbent former sheriff.

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 30th Legislative District (Federal Way and nearby south King County communities), serving a two-year term in the Washington State House.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Jamila E. Taylor, chair of the House Civil Rights & Judiciary Committee, faces Republican first-time candidate Tiffany Bowyer, a small business owner running on affordability and spending accountability. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state representative seeking a third term, chair of the House Civil Rights & Judiciary Committee and an attorney with a legal-aid and victim-advocacy background.
highlights: Cites passing laws on gun-violence reduction, police training and trust, small-business tax reduction, and working-family tax credit expansion [S1]
Campaign highlights homeownership-access legislation and remediation of racially restrictive property deeds [S2]
Endorsed by the 30th District Democrats, State Labor Council, Planned Parenthood, OneAmerica Votes, and Sierra Club; campaign site cites a Seattle Times endorsement [S1][S2]

experience.basis: Third-term incumbent and committee chair with a cited record of passed legislation; opponent is a first-time candidate.

safety.basis: Record emphasizes root causes of crime, gun-violence reduction, and police training/trust reform rather than enforcement-first measures.

taxes.basis: Record is targeted relief in both directions — small-business tax reductions and expanded working-family tax credits — with no stated position on new progressive revenue.

housing.basis: Emphasizes public resources for housing, a new homeownership program, and remediation of discriminatory deed covenants.

spending.basis: Platform leads with delivering resources for housing, childcare, and expanded tax credits — an investment orientation.

social.basis: Chairs the Civil Rights & Judiciary Committee and vice-chairs the Members of Color Caucus; endorsed by Planned Parenthood and OneAmerica Votes.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate from Federal Way, a small business owner with a health-and-wellness background running on affordability, spending accountability, and police staffing.
highlights: Opposes new taxes on working families and businesses; says government spending growth is driving up property taxes, gas prices, and utility bills [S1][S2]
Calls for transparent tracking of taxpayer funds and program performance audits, citing untracked childcare funding and grant-oversight gaps [S2]
Proposes fully staffing police departments and holding repeat offenders accountable, coordinated with behavioral-health services [S2]

taxes.basis: Repeated explicit opposition to new taxes on working families and businesses across pamphlet and campaign site.

spending.basis: Names spending growth as the driver of costs and calls specifically for transparent fund tracking and program performance audits.

safety.basis: Leads with fully staffing police and repeat-offender accountability, tempered by proposed coordination with behavioral-health services.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected experience or legislative record, running against an incumbent committee chair.

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 31st Legislative District, serving a two-year term voting on state law and the operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Republican incumbent Drew Stokesbary, the sitting House Minority Leader, faces Democrat Stephen Szczurko-Walton, a first-time candidate, Navy veteran, and HVAC technician running on campaign-finance reform. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Republican state representative from Auburn and the current House Minority Leader, an attorney advising startups and small businesses on corporate and securities law.
highlights: Selected House Republican leader in 2023; previously Ranking Member on House Appropriations and House Minority Floor Leader [S1][S3]
Says taxes "keep increasing, by billions each year" and called the 2026 session "an inflection point" given another budget shortfall [S1][S4]
Campaign site lists endorsements from the Washington Council of Police and Sheriffs, Washington State PTA, and State Council of Fire Fighters [S2]

experience.basis: Sitting House Minority Leader with a long legislative tenure and prior Appropriations ranking-member and floor-leader roles.

taxes.basis: Repeated explicit critique of rising state taxes, including citing billions in new 2025 taxes and fees at the 2026 session opening.

safety.basis: Pledges "safer neighborhoods where laws are enforced and criminals are apprehended" — an enforcement-first framing with no prevention component.

candidates.summary: Democratic first-time candidate, a U.S. Navy nuclear reactor operator from 2015-2024 and now a commercial HVAC technician, challenging the sitting House Minority Leader.
highlights: Signature issue is passing SB 6358 to "end corporate election spending in Washington," framed as a way to bypass Citizens United [S1][S2]
Pledges to "defend the Climate Commitment Act," modernize the electrical grid, and maintain diverse clean energy options [S1][S2]
Website advocates universal healthcare and progressive taxation to bring down housing, healthcare, and tax costs [S1][S2]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no prior elected experience, running a challenger campaign against the House Minority Leader.

reform.basis: Makes ending corporate election spending (SB 6358) his signature issue across pamphlet and website.

climate.basis: Explicitly pledges to defend the Climate Commitment Act and expand clean energy, repeated in both pamphlet and website.

healthcare.basis: Website advocates universal healthcare.

taxes.basis: Website advocates progressive taxation while pledging to bring down tax costs for regular households — a burden-shifting posture.

contests.office_does: One of two House members elected from the district to write state law and adopt Washington's operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: An open seat vacated by 16-year incumbent Cindy Ryu drew five Democrats — three sitting city councilmembers and two first-time candidates with professional-expertise pitches — plus one Republican, Lisa Rezac. With the Democratic vote split five ways in a top-two primary, the real contest is which Democrat advances, and the sharpest divide among them is tax approach.

candidates.summary: Shoreline City Councilmember since 2016 and former mayor, with a career spanning paramedic, King County deputy prosecutor, UN war crimes prosecutor, and environmental-nonprofit legal director.
highlights: Supports restructuring the state tax code to mirror the federal progressive system and taxing business profit rather than revenue [S2][S3][S4]
Proposes addressing the state budget deficit with across-the-board cuts sparing education [S3]
Supports expanding affordable housing, including upzoning near transit; cites a Shoreline record on housing, transit, and fielding mental health professionals [S1][S2][S3]
Campaigns on fighting climate change through electrification of fleets, homes, and businesses; endorsed by Sierra Club Washington [S2][S3][S6]
The only candidate endorsed by both the Seattle Times and The Stranger editorial boards, plus retiring Rep. Cindy Ryu [S1][S2][S3][S4]

taxes.basis: Repeatedly proposes progressive restructuring of the state tax code, including taxing business profit rather than revenue.

spending.basis: Proposes across-the-board budget cuts except education to address the deficit — an explicit restraint plank unusual in this Democratic field.

housing.basis: Mixed approach: upzoning near transit (supply-side) alongside expanding affordable housing investment in his Shoreline record.

safety.basis: Cites bringing mental health professionals into the field in Shoreline; a services-alongside-response record rather than enforcement-first.

climate.basis: Campaigns on fighting climate change via broad electrification and carries the Sierra Club Washington endorsement.

experience.basis: Longest local-government tenure in the field: planning commission since 2010, council since 2016, deputy mayor and mayor, endorsed by the retiring incumbent.

candidates.summary: First-time candidate and attorney with roughly 20 years at the Federal Trade Commission, most recently on the antitrust case against Meta; the race's top fundraiser.
highlights: Core platform is challenging monopoly power and corporate consolidation in tech, healthcare, and groceries, framed as lowering consumer prices [S1][S2]
Proposes tech guardrails for children and workers: ed-tech data limits, regulating AI in classrooms, banning school cellphones, and preventing surveillance pricing [S1][S2][S3]
Prioritizes fully funding public schools and moving away from per-student head-count funding [S2][S3][S4]
Advocates budget transparency and examining state liability in lawsuit settlements as a cost-cutting measure [S4]
Top fundraiser by a wide margin, with the Washington State Labor Council and a bloc of legislators behind her [S1][S2][S5]

spending.basis: Mixed: pairs fully funding public schools with budget transparency and settlement-liability review as cost-cutting.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record, though her pitch is federal enforcement expertise rather than outsider renewal.

tech.basis: Tech/AI regulation is her core platform — ed-tech data limits, classroom AI rules, surveillance-pricing bans — backed by FTC antitrust litigation experience.

candidates.summary: First-time candidate, recruiting executive, and founder of a climate-focused recruiting company, recruited to run by Rep. Lauren Davis and endorsed by both sitting LD32 legislators.
highlights: Supports the state's millionaires tax but opposes raising taxes on working families [S4]
Campaigns on growing Washington's green economy through clean energy investment and solar/wind development [S2][S3]
Prioritizes fully funding public schools and detaching school funding from student head counts [S3][S4]
Testified in support of legislation to 'demask ICE' and champions immigrant communities [S1]
Endorsed by Sen. Salomon, Rep. Davis, and the Washington Council of Police and Sheriffs — a law-enforcement endorsement uncommon in this Democratic field [S1][S3][S4]

taxes.basis: Supports keeping the millionaires tax while opposing new taxes on working families — pro-progressive-revenue but not expansionist.

spending.basis: Mixed: leads with fully funding public schools while pledging to scrutinize spending and expect results.

safety.basis: Endorsed by the Washington Council of Police and Sheriffs and leads first-responder roundtables on Aurora Avenue safety, while pledging evidence-based homelessness approaches.

climate.basis: Founded a climate-focused recruiting company and campaigns centrally on clean-energy investment and green-economy growth.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elective record, though recruited and backed by the district's sitting legislators.

immigration.basis: Testified in support of 'demask ICE' legislation and campaigns on support for immigrant communities.

social.basis: States 'women's rights are paramount' and champions civil rights.

tech.basis: Pledges to author AI legislation protecting children.

candidates.summary: Two-term Edmonds City Councilmember, licensed CPA, and former Fortune 500 auditor who chaired the council's Finance Committee and leads with fiscal-management credentials.
highlights: Wants to remove the 1% property tax cap so revenue can match inflation, while describing himself as a social liberal who is financially 'careful' [S4]
Brought cost-reduction proposals during Edmonds' 2024 budget difficulties, including reduced city hall hours and police-leadership restructuring [S6]
Supports housing and homelessness solutions combining affordable housing with wraparound services [S3]
Re-elected to the Edmonds council in 2025 with 88.3% of the vote [S1][S6]
Endorsements come mostly from local mayors and councilmembers — plus former U.S. Rep. Brian Baird — rather than state legislators, labor, or newspapers [S2][S3]

taxes.basis: Supports lifting the 1% property tax cap so revenue tracks inflation, framed cautiously rather than as broad progressive-revenue expansion.

spending.basis: CPA/auditor profile, self-described financial carefulness, and a council record of concrete cost-reduction proposals during a budget crunch.

housing.basis: Backs affordable housing paired with wraparound services — a public-investment framing rather than deregulation.

experience.basis: Two-term sitting councilmember and finance chair with a governance record, though no legislative tenure.

candidates.summary: Edmonds City Councilmember since 2022 and business attorney, running on progressive revenue and affordability shaped by her caregiving and immigrant-family experience.
highlights: Supports repealing the 1% property tax cap, has floated a flat state income tax, and calls for 'progressive revenue' [S2][S3]
Supports affordable homeownership including state land trusts, and argues 'building more housing is not enough' without dedicated social-services funding [S1][S2][S3]
Pledges to protect Apple Health (Medicaid) and state health programs from federal threats [S2]
Says she is the only candidate endorsed by the teachers' union via WEA-PAC; also endorsed by Snohomish County Executive Dave Somers [S1][S2]
Backs climate resiliency measures, citing increasingly common extreme weather [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Supports repealing the 1% property tax cap, has floated an income tax, and campaigns on 'progressive revenue.'

spending.basis: Consistent investment platform: dedicated social-services funding for homelessness, statewide school funding, and subsidized college costs.

housing.basis: Backs state land trusts for affordable homeownership and says building more housing is not enough without social services.

climate.basis: Supports climate resiliency measures, citing extreme weather; no carbon-pricing position in the dossier.

experience.basis: Sitting councilmember since 2022 (appointed, then elected; Council President Pro Tem), a shorter governing record than the field's most tenured.

immigration.basis: Pro bono work with the Northwest Immigrant Rights Project; no stated enforcement-policy position.

healthcare.basis: Pledges to protect Apple Health/Medicaid within existing structures from federal threats; no system-transformation agenda stated.

candidates.summary: The race's only Republican, a fundraising auctioneer and former LD32/King County GOP officer who lost to Rep. Ryu in 2024 with about 31% and runs against one-party control.
highlights: Campaign slogan 'Less Waste. Lower Taxes. Better Results.'; opposes new taxes and fee increases [S2][S3]
Pledges to expose 'waste, fraud, and mismanagement,' with independent audits of major state programs [S1][S2][S3]
Campaigns on parental rights in education and 'restoring excellence' [S1][S3]
Prioritizes safer communities and support for law enforcement [S1][S3]
Raised $4,058, the least in the race, and lists no endorsements [S1][S2][S4]

taxes.basis: Opposes new taxes and fee increases and advocates tax relief as her campaign slogan's centerpiece.

spending.basis: Platform is organized around exposing waste and fraud, independent audits of major state programs, and zero tolerance for misuse of funds.

safety.basis: Prioritizes safer communities and support for law enforcement; no specific enforcement proposals in the dossier.

experience.basis: No elective office record; runs explicitly against one-party control, though she is a longtime party organizer and repeat candidate.

social.basis: Campaigns on parental rights in education; no further social-policy specifics in the dossier.

contests.office_does: One of two House members elected from the district to write state law and adopt Washington's operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Edwin Obras, who narrowly won a 2025 special election, faces two first-time Republican challengers — Chris Martinez, who has the King County GOP endorsement and a detailed conservative platform, and Darryl K. Jones, who runs a one-theme fiscal-restraint message. The primary effectively decides which Republican joins Obras on the November ballot.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democrat and Assistant Majority Whip, a Filipino immigrant and 18-year Seattle Human Services Department veteran who won the seat by 641 votes in the 2025 special election.
highlights: Champions rent-increase caps and anti-displacement housing legislation, framing affordability around rising rents [S1][S2]
Six bills signed into law in his first full session, including isolated-worker protections, strengthened firearm background checks, and rideshare regulation [S1][S2]
Pledges to continue 'fighting for immigrant rights' [S1]
Says he secured over $30 million in state investments for the district, including housing and health projects [S1]
Backed by the Washington State Labor Council, Planned Parenthood, Washington Conservation Action, and Gov. Ferguson [S1]

spending.basis: Frames state investment as the affordability answer — $30 million secured for district projects and expanded mental health resources — with no restraint planks.

housing.basis: Champions rent-increase caps and anti-displacement legislation as his core housing agenda.

safety.basis: Focuses on jail reform and community safety standards, alongside background-check and police-impersonation laws.

climate.basis: Endorsed by Washington Conservation Action; no stated climate-policy position in the dossier.

experience.basis: Incumbent with six bills signed in his first full session, an Assistant Majority Whip post, and 18 years in city human-services leadership.

immigration.basis: Pledges in his pamphlet statement to continue 'fighting for immigrant rights.'

social.basis: Endorsed by Planned Parenthood; no detailed social-policy statements in the dossier.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate and blue-collar tradesman with the King County GOP endorsement, running the most detailed conservative platform in the race.
highlights: Would work to eliminate the Climate Commitment Act, which he blames for high energy costs, and pledges to 'abolish energy-killing taxes' [S1][S2]
Wants lower taxes — especially B&O taxes — and reduced spending and regulation [S2]
Supports increased law-enforcement presence and holding criminals accountable [S1][S2]
Opposes 'woke ideologies' in schools without parental consent; runs on parental rights and 'traditional family values' [S1][S2]
Names specific bills he opposes: HB 2165, SB 5925, and HB 2320, which he calls constitutional violations, plus SB 5974 concerning elected sheriffs [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Pledges to lower taxes, especially B&O taxes, and to 'abolish energy-killing taxes.'

spending.basis: Wants to reduce wasteful spending and regulation as a core plank.

safety.basis: Supports increased law-enforcement presence and holding criminals accountable.

climate.basis: Explicitly targets the Climate Commitment Act for elimination, blaming it for high energy costs.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record who calls for replacing career politicians with limited-government leaders.

social.basis: Runs on parental rights, 'traditional family values,' religious liberty, and opposition to 'woke ideologies' in schools.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate from Kent with a career in customer-facing healthcare and service roles, running a single-theme fiscal-restraint message.
highlights: Core message: 'Washington has a spending problem'; pledges never to vote to increase constituents' costs [S1]
Runs on 'policy over party,' arguing one-party dominance has not benefited voters [S1]
No endorsements, itemized fundraising, or policy positions beyond the pamphlet statement were found [S1][S2][S3]

taxes.basis: Criticizes Democratic majorities for higher taxes and pledges never to vote to increase constituents' costs.

spending.basis: His core message is that Washington has a spending problem requiring restraint.

experience.basis: First-time candidate whose only political role is precinct committee officer, running against one-party dominance.

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 34th District (West Seattle, Vashon Island, White Center, west Burien) in the 98-member Washington House, serving two-year terms writing state law and budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Brianna K. Thomas (D), appointed in January 2025 to fill Emily Alvarado's House seat and confirmed in the November 2025 special election, is the only candidate filed and advances unopposed on the primary ballot.

candidates.summary: First-term incumbent State Representative, appointed in January 2025 and confirmed by voters that November; a labor organizer and former Seattle City Hall chief of staff with a democracy-reform background.
highlights: Managed the 2013 SeaTac $15 minimum wage campaign and led Seattle's Honest Elections initiative that created the democracy voucher program [S1][S3]
Says she passed bills guaranteeing meal and rest breaks for healthcare workers and protections for domestic workers, and contributed to passing the 'Millionaires Tax' [S1]
Former Chief of Staff to Seattle City Council President Lorena González and Mayor's Office labor liaison; now a Senior Policy Advisor at Seattle's Human Services Department [S1][S4]

taxes.basis: Says she contributed to passing the 'Millionaires Tax,' describing it as tax reform benefiting working Washingtonians.

housing.basis: Former field director for the Washington Low Income Housing Alliance Action Fund; affordable housing is a recurring theme of her organizing career.

experience.basis: First-term legislator, but with a long insider career (City Hall chief of staff, mayoral labor liaison) and bills passed — a record-leaning profile rather than an outsider one.

reform.basis: Led Seattle's Honest Elections initiative creating the democracy voucher program — a concrete structural campaign-finance reform record.

social.basis: Says she passed legislation ensuring employees' privacy and bodily autonomy.

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 36th District (Ballard, Magnolia, Queen Anne, Greenwood area of northwest Seattle), serving two-year terms writing state law and budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Julia Grant Reed, first elected in 2022, is the only candidate filed and advances unopposed on the primary ballot.

candidates.summary: Second-term incumbent State Representative and workforce-development consultant with prior policy experience in the Obama administration and Seattle city government.
highlights: Distinguishing focus is workforce development: investing in apprenticeship pathways, youth employment, and higher education, drawn from her consulting career [S1]
Prior service as Senior Policy Advisor & Regional Affairs Director for the City of Seattle and in the Obama administration at the State Department and OMB [S1]
Pamphlet endorsements include the State Labor Council, Planned Parenthood Alliance Advocates, Alliance for Gun Responsibility, and WA Nurses Association PAC [S1]

climate.basis: Pledges to be a strong voice for climate-friendly transportation solutions.

experience.basis: Second-term incumbent with federal (Obama administration) and City of Seattle policy experience — a record-based profile with moderate tenure.

social.basis: Endorsement pattern includes Planned Parenthood Alliance Advocates and Alliance for Gun Responsibility.

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 37th District (southeast Seattle, Skyway, part of Renton) in the 98-member Washington House, voting on state law, budget, and taxes.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Sharon Tomiko Santos, in office since 1999 and chair of the House Education Committee, faces first-time candidate Kelabe Tewolde, who is running on generational change. Both prefer the Democratic Party, overlap broadly on policy, and advance to the general; the practical contrast is seniority versus new leadership.

candidates.summary: Incumbent State Representative since 1999 and chair of the House Education Committee; the first Japanese American woman elected to a mainland state legislature, with deep district and nonprofit roots.
highlights: Roughly 27 years of incumbency and the House Education Committee chairmanship — the deepest institutional seniority in this race [S1][S3]
As Education Chair, focused on accessible quality education, a 'fundamental reimagining' of education delivery, childcare affordability, and workforce pathways [S1][S2]
Deep district roots: Franklin High School alumna, founder of three nonprofits, founding board member of OneAmerica [S1]

taxes.basis: Pamphlet cites 'tax fairness for working families' without further specifics.

housing.basis: Supports both increasing housing supply and expanding affordable housing near transit while preventing displacement — a mixed supply-plus-protections posture.

safety.basis: Campaign emphasizes community-based violence prevention and expanded behavioral health services.

experience.basis: Serving since 1999 with a committee chairmanship — a maximal seniority and institutional-record profile.

immigration.basis: Founding board member of OneAmerica (Hate Free Zone), an immigrant-advocacy organization — an indirect signal; no stated policy position.

candidates.summary: First-time candidate, Rainier Scholars leadership director, and Seattle Planning Commissioner running on 'a new generation of leadership,' with a platform shaped by childhood experience of housing insecurity and medical debt.
highlights: Challenger running explicitly on generational change against a roughly 27-year incumbent [S1][S3]
Seattle Planning Commissioner since 2020 and Associate Director of Leadership Development at Rainier Scholars — policy experience without prior elected office [S1]
Priorities include housing affordability, fully funding early childhood education, interest limits on medical debt, and community-based safety investment [S1][S2]

spending.basis: Calls for fully funding early childhood education and public investment in historically neglected areas like South Seattle and Skyway.

safety.basis: Advocates community-based safety built on public investment rather than enforcement-led approaches.

experience.basis: First-time candidate whose campaign message centers on 'a new generation of leadership' and lived experience.

contests.office_does: One of two House members elected from the district to write state law and adopt Washington's operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Recently appointed-then-elected Democratic incumbent Janice Zahn holds a large resource and endorsement advantage over Republican Elle Nguyen, who offers the sole policy alternative on fiscal restraint and parental rights, and perennial candidate Alex Tsimerman, whose statement contains no policy content. The primary decides which challenger joins Zahn on the November ballot.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democrat, civil engineer, and Port of Seattle Chief Engineer who served on the Bellevue City Council 2017-2025 and won the seat in a November 2025 special election after appointment.
highlights: Focused on 'improving affordability and delivering investments in housing, education, childcare, and transportation' [S1]
First-term bills signed into law span healthcare-provider accountability, public-works contracting, transportation safety, and a building-code change adding housing flexibility [S2][S3]
Vice-Chair of the House Local Government Committee; also sits on Housing, Finance, and Transportation [S1]
Says she aims to address state budget challenges 'responsibly' while protecting essential services [S1][S2]
Broad institutional endorsements: nurses and teachers unions, Planned Parenthood, Washington Conservation Action, Sierra Club, and sitting officials [S1][S2][S7]

spending.basis: Leads with delivering public investments in housing, education, childcare, and transportation, tempered by 'responsible' budget framing.

housing.basis: Frames housing as an investment priority, alongside a code-streamlining bill (HB 2228) adding supply flexibility.

climate.basis: Lists environmental protection among priorities and is endorsed by Washington Conservation Action and Sierra Club Washington.

experience.basis: Incumbent with a first-term bill record, eight years on the Bellevue council, and a career as Port of Seattle Chief Engineer.

social.basis: Endorsed by Planned Parenthood; equity- and access-oriented framing, without detailed social-policy statements in the dossier.

candidates.summary: Republican small business owner and law-school graduate from Mercer Island, making her third run for public office on fiscal restraint, public safety, and parental rights.
highlights: Priorities are 'stopping wasteful spending, balancing the budget, and protecting taxpayers from unnecessary tax increases' [S2]
Supports law enforcement and neighborhood safety [S1][S2]
Emphasizes parental rights, 'traditional values,' protecting children, and strengthening families [S1][S2]
Calls for open government, honest accounting, and accountability [S1][S2]
No reported fundraising or organizational endorsements found as of mid-July 2026 [S2][S5]

taxes.basis: Pledges to protect taxpayers from 'unnecessary tax increases' — restraint-oriented, though hedged rather than a blanket no-new-taxes stance.

spending.basis: Platform centers on stopping wasteful spending, balancing the budget, and honest accounting.

safety.basis: Supports law enforcement and neighborhood safety; no specific enforcement proposals in the dossier.

experience.basis: Has never held elected office; runs on community advocacy after unsuccessful 2021 and 2022 bids.

social.basis: Campaigns on parental rights and 'traditional values,' the pamphlet and website's central family-policy theme.

candidates.summary: Perennial candidate running under the 'Standup-America Party' label whose pamphlet statement consists of slogans and submits no policy positions, experience, or qualifications.
highlights: Pamphlet statement is a series of slogans with no policy proposals for the office [S1]
Multiple PDC enforcement cases across 2021-2024 cycles for unfiled disclosure reports, with fines noted as unpaid [S3][S4]
No 2026 fundraising records, endorsements, or submitted qualifications found [S1][S6]

experience.basis: No elected or governmental record; a repeat candidate who submits no qualifications, placing him at the no-record pole without an articulated renewal case.

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for LD 43 (central Seattle); votes on state law, the state budget, and taxes.
contests.race_blurb: Both candidates prefer the Democratic Party and both advance to the general regardless of the primary result. A nine-year incumbent with a housing-policy record faces a first-time challenger whose platform is prospective.

candidates.summary: Incumbent state representative since 2017 and DESC deputy director, with a legislative record centered on housing, homelessness, and renter protections.
highlights: Lead campaigner for HB 1217, the 2025 rent stabilization bill limiting excessive rent increases [S3]
Deputy director at DESC, overseeing supportive housing and services for people who have experienced homelessness [S1]
Cites 'historic tax fairness' measures funding schools and childcare; says she is voting no on all statewide initiatives on this ballot [S1]

taxes.basis: Touts 'historic tax fairness' measures funding schools and childcare as accomplishments.

spending.basis: Runs on revenue measures funding schools, childcare, and services; investment-oriented framing.

housing.basis: Lead campaigner for HB 1217 rent stabilization; career in supportive housing at DESC; 2026 focus on renter protections.

safety.basis: Gun-violence reduction and fentanyl-crisis work paired with a housing-first professional background at DESC.

climate.basis: States priorities of tackling climate change and climate-resilient infrastructure.

experience.basis: Incumbent since 2017 with an enacted housing record and homelessness-services leadership.

immigration.basis: States work protecting immigrant residents from federal overreach.

healthcare.basis: Focus on healthcare affordability and expanding access to affordable insurance within existing structures.

social.basis: States work protecting LGBTQ+ residents from federal overreach.

candidates.summary: First-time candidate and UW resident director who self-describes as a 'pragmatic progressive Democrat'; his platform is entirely prospective.
highlights: Top stated priority is reducing corporate influence in politics [S1]
Supports regulating artificial intelligence to prevent unfair pricing [S1]
UW resident director overseeing operations and safety for nearly 10,000 residents; no legislative or governmental record [S1][S2]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no legislative or governmental record; platform is entirely prospective.

reform.basis: Names reducing corporate influence in politics as a stated top priority.

tech.basis: Supports regulating artificial intelligence to prevent unfair pricing.

contests.office_does: One of two House members for LD 45 (Kirkland, Woodinville area); writes and votes on state laws and the state operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Both candidates advance to the general regardless of the primary result. A nearly 20-year Democratic incumbent who chairs the House Community Safety Committee faces a first-time Republican candidate running on election security, parental rights, and deregulation.

candidates.summary: Incumbent state representative since 2007 with a legal and criminal-justice policy background; chairs the House Community Safety Committee.
highlights: Cites Washington's police accountability reform package and expanded drug and mental health services as signature work [S1][S2]
Chairs the House Community Safety and Statute Law committees; legislator since 2007 [S1][S3]
Supports climate action including carbon-emissions legislation, continued K-12 funding, and free school meals [S1][S2]

experience.basis: Incumbent since 2007 with committee chairmanships and a long legislative record.

safety.basis: Record of police accountability reform, expanded drug and mental health services, and helping people in crisis.

climate.basis: Supports climate action; cites carbon-emissions cap legislation as an accomplishment.

housing.basis: Supports affordable housing investments (public-investment framing).

spending.basis: Runs on delivered investments: free school meals, affordable childcare, continued K-12 funding.

social.basis: Pledges to champion reproductive rights.

candidates.summary: Retired music teacher and compliance professional making her first legislative run; presents herself as 'not a politician' after a 2025 Northshore School Board candidacy.
highlights: Platform centers voter ID, paper ballots, and bipartisan election oversight framed as 'secure elections' [S1][S2]
Wants to empower parents in education; opposes critical race theory and gender ideology in classrooms [S1][S2]
Champions affordable energy including fossil fuels and nuclear, and would fight housing regulations she views as cost drivers [S1][S2]

experience.basis: First-time legislative candidate presenting herself as 'not a politician'; no elected public office.

housing.basis: Would fight regulations she views as driving up housing costs; supports affordability through zoning reform, not tenant protections.

climate.basis: Champions affordable energy including fossil fuels and nuclear power.

safety.basis: Supports 'full support for our law enforcement,' paired with treatment-focused recovery programs for homelessness.

social.basis: Repeatedly emphasizes parental empowerment in schools and opposition to critical race theory and gender ideology in classrooms.

spending.basis: Emphasizes transparency, fiscal accountability, and eliminating government waste.

contests.office_does: A state representative serves a two-year term in Washington's 98-member House, writing and voting on state law and the operating, capital, and transportation budgets — schools, housing, taxes, healthcare, and public safety.
contests.race_blurb: Seven-term incumbent Gerry Pollet faces his first serious challenge since 2012 from two well-funded first-time candidates, Ron Davis and Will Dreher; all three are Democrats, and AI regulation and campaign funding have become the race's flashpoints.

candidates.summary: The incumbent since 2011, a public-interest attorney and UW public health faculty member who chairs the Legislature's audit committee (JLARC) and centers education funding and open government.
highlights: Fifteen years in the House [S1][S7]; chairs JLARC and sits on Appropriations [S3]; helped lift the special-education funding cap and co-developed the Washington College Grant [S2]
Says he championed the 'millionaires' and capital gains taxes and wants to cut the sales tax to reduce regressive reliance [S1]
Signature open-government record: Washington Coalition for Open Government board, 'Sunshine in Government' honoree, conflict-of-interest and public-records legislation [S2]
Cites passing 'ICE mask bans,' funding abortion access, and strengthening hate-crime laws [S1]
Backed by Gov. Ferguson, Rep. Jayapal, and labor, but outraised by both challengers this cycle [S6]

taxes.basis: Claims authorship-level roles on millionaires and capital gains taxes and advocates progressive tax reform to cut sales-tax reliance.

spending.basis: Chairs the Legislature's audit committee and pushes corporate tax-preference accountability while championing major education funding expansion.

housing.basis: Backs removing density barriers and transit-oriented development alongside inclusionary affordable-unit requirements — a blend of both poles.

climate.basis: Career environmental-cleanup leadership at Hanford, Washington Conservation Action endorsement, and microplastics-reduction legislation.

experience.basis: Seven-term incumbent with committee chairmanship, Appropriations seniority, and an enacted legislative record.

reform.basis: Long transparency record — open-government board service, public-records fights, and conflict-of-interest standards for legislators.

immigration.basis: Cites passing 'ICE mask bans' and protecting immigrants — matching the constrain-federal-enforcement pole.

social.basis: Funded abortion access, emphasizes reproductive freedom and LGBTQ+ rights, and strengthened hate-crime laws.

candidates.summary: A startup founder, housing-policy expert, and urbanist activist who narrowly lost a 2023 Seattle council race, backed by Seattle Mayor Katie Wilson and the housing movement.
highlights: The race's urbanist candidate: build enough homes to reduce costs, invest in transit, and freeze rent in publicly owned housing [S2]
Says he helped pass major state housing bills and defended the 'millionaire tax' funding Seattle Social Housing against Amazon's repeal effort [S1]
Would tax wealthy individuals and corporations to fund schools, universal healthcare, and free childcare [S1]
Added AI consumer and worker protections to his platform and attacks Dreher's AI-industry funding as compromising independence [S4]
Endorsed by Seattle Mayor Katie Wilson, housing-movement leaders, and the Transit Riders Union [S2]

taxes.basis: Platform is taxing wealthy individuals and corporations to fund public goods; defended the Seattle Social Housing millionaire tax.

spending.basis: Public investment is his primary lever: free childcare for all, transit investment, and funding universal public goods.

housing.basis: Spans both poles: supply-abundance urbanism (build enough homes) combined with social-housing advocacy and public-housing rent freezes.

climate.basis: Transit investment is a stated priority and he does environmental-justice work on the Futurewise board; no direct carbon-policy stance in the dossier.

experience.basis: Never held elected office and runs as a change candidate, though with a movement record of coalition wins and a prior council campaign.

reform.basis: Makes campaign-money independence an issue, attacking Dreher's AI-industry and out-of-state funding; organized grassroots corporate-taxation campaigns.

healthcare.basis: Lists funding 'universal healthcare' among the public goods his tax platform would pay for.

tech.basis: Prioritizes protecting consumers and workers from AI-related harms, a plank added after Dreher entered the race.

candidates.summary: A first-time candidate and former federal prosecutor (January 6th cases, Kagan clerkship) making aggressive AI, data-center, and social-media regulation his campaign's centerpiece; endorsed by The Stranger.
highlights: The only candidate promising to 'aggressively regulate tech' — AI disinformation, automation job loss, data-center growth, and social-media safeguards for kids [S1]
Former Assistant U.S. Attorney who prosecuted about 25 January 6th defendants, hate crimes, and civil-rights violations by officers; clerked for Justice Kagan [S1]
Race's top fundraiser (~$263K); roughly $100K traces to AI-industry donors, which opponents cite to question his independence [S4]
Former 4th-grade teacher proposing to fully fund schools; would fix the regressive tax structure while holding government accountable for tax dollars [S1]
Endorsed by The Stranger [S6], Seattle City Attorney Erika Evans, and Councilmember Dionne Foster [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Pledges to fix Washington's regressive tax structure, without the structural-tax specifics his rivals offer.

housing.basis: Frames housing as both cutting red tape and funding affordable housing — brief pamphlet statements pointing to both poles.

safety.basis: Career prosecutor promising 'evidence-based public safety' and to hold criminals accountable; endorsed by a former mayoral public-safety chief.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no legislative or elected experience who entered in April 2026 and runs on a fresh professional lens.

tech.basis: Signature issue: aggressive AI, data-center, and social-media regulation as the centerpiece of his platform.

spending.basis: His pamphlet explicitly pairs both poles: 'hold government accountable for tax dollars' (audit/restrain) alongside fully funding schools and funding affordable housing, childcare, and transit (invest/expand). The scoring file's own highlight quotes this language yet no spending score was assigned; comparable mixed evidence earned Pollet a 0/medium.

contests.office_does: A state representative serves a two-year term in Washington's 98-member House, writing and voting on state law and budgets covering school funding, housing, taxes, public safety, and healthcare.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Debra Entenman faces three challengers in a South King County swing district: Democratic newcomer Jasnoor Kaur Hans, self-styled 'Cascade Democrat' Logan Evans, and Libertarian Cobi Clark, who is endorsed by the district's Republicans. Public safety and addiction policy is the field's sharpest split.

candidates.summary: A Kent-raised legal advocate and political newcomer working in family law, with disability and immigrant-advocacy experience, running on housing, root-causes public safety, and generational change.
highlights: Housing platform centers public tools: expand the state Housing Trust Fund, fund tenant right-to-counsel, and support community land trusts [S2]
The field's clearest root-causes public-safety candidate, explicitly rejecting sentencing increases in favor of housing, mental-health services, and youth programs [S2]
Legal-advocacy background with the Washington Autism Alliance and immigration case support for Kids in Need of Defense [S1]
Frames the race against 'career politicians'; top fundraiser in the race at about $37K, more than double the incumbent [S3]

taxes.basis: Says she will 'make the tax system fairer' — directional but unspecified.

spending.basis: Platform is investment-forward: trust-fund expansion, funded right-to-counsel, more school counselors and mental-health staff.

housing.basis: Explicit public-tools platform: Housing Trust Fund expansion, tenant right-to-counsel, and community land trusts.

safety.basis: Explicitly rejects increased sentencing in favor of root causes — stable housing, mental-health services, youth programs.

experience.basis: Political newcomer in her first race, running against 'career politicians' on generational change.

immigration.basis: Platform defends immigrant protections and language access; volunteer immigration case support with Kids in Need of Defense.

healthcare.basis: Focuses on lowering out-of-pocket costs and holding insurers and hospital pricing accountable within the current system.

social.basis: Platform defends reproductive freedom, civil rights, and due process.

candidates.summary: A Libertarian housing developer and construction project manager, endorsed by both his party and the 47th District Republicans, running on supply-side housing, fiscal restraint, and civil liberties.
highlights: Argues from developer experience that excessive regulation, rising taxes, and bureaucratic delay drive up housing costs [S1]
Runs on fiscal responsibility, accountable government, and continued opposition to a state income tax [S2]
Calls for abundant, reliable, affordable energy and ties energy costs to regulation [S2]
Endorsed by the 47th District Republicans and the Libertarian Party, likely consolidating the right-of-center vote with no Republican filed [S2]
Lost an Auburn City Council race in 2025; raised about $19.5K this cycle [S4]

taxes.basis: Continued opposition to any state income tax and a limited-government fiscal platform.

spending.basis: Fiscal responsibility and accountable government are core planks, organized under 'Enact, Reform, and Repeal.'

housing.basis: Supply-side deregulation from hands-on developer experience; blames regulation, taxes, and bureaucratic delay for housing costs.

climate.basis: Energy platform is abundance and affordability, tying energy costs to regulation; no clean-energy or carbon-pricing planks.

experience.basis: Has never held elected office; a first-time legislative candidate after losing a 2025 Auburn council race.

candidates.summary: A Kent- and Auburn-area public school teacher running under a self-defined 'Cascade Democrat' label as a results-first alternative, proposing high-rise transit housing and a mandatory-rehab three-strikes policy.
highlights: Proposes a 'three-strikes' policy with six months of mandatory rehab — the most coercive treatment stance in the field [S1]
Supports high-rise housing near light rail to lower housing costs [S1]
Defines 'Cascade Democrat' as 'actionable rational solutions that don't waste your tax dollars,' pairing compassion with competence [S2]
Teacher at an alternative public high school; volunteers with addiction-recovery and homeless-housing groups; endorsed by ATU Local 587 [S1]

spending.basis: Runs on efficient use of tax dollars and results-first governance — 'solutions that don't waste your tax dollars.'

housing.basis: Housing answer is dense market supply — high-rise development near light rail to lower costs — without public-tools planks.

safety.basis: Proposes a three-strikes framework with six months of mandatory rehab, the field's most coercive treatment stance.

experience.basis: First-time legislative candidate positioning against the incumbent; lost a 2025 Kent council primary.

candidates.summary: The incumbent Democrat since 2018, a former congressional district director with signed legislation on public-safety funding and college-student basic needs, sitting on four House committees.
highlights: Prime sponsor of HB 2015 (2025), which her pamphlet says secured $100 million for public safety with a local-option tax [S4]
Prime sponsor of HB 1559 (2023), creating benefits navigators for college students' basic needs [S5]
Public-safety approach pairs funding with accountability: independent deadly-force investigations, anti-bias hiring standards, and social workers in crisis response [S2]
Would expand TANF, eviction-prevention assistance, childcare, and healthcare access [S2]
Outraised by all three challengers this cycle at about $16K [S6]

taxes.basis: Sponsored and passed a local-option tax to fund public safety — new revenue authority rather than tax limitation.

spending.basis: Platform expands safety-net programs — TANF, eviction prevention, childcare — as the primary lever.

housing.basis: Emphasizes eviction-prevention assistance and safety-net supports from her Housing Committee seat; no deregulation planks.
