safety.basis: Pairs $100M in new public-safety funding with accountability measures and social-worker crisis response — a documented blend of both poles.

experience.basis: Eight years in the seat, verifiable signed legislation, four committee assignments, and prior congressional staff service.

tech.basis: Consumer data-rights platform: mandatory disclosure of data collection with consumer control and informed consent.

contests.office_does: One of two House members for LD 48 (Redmond, Bellevue, Kirkland area); serves a two-year term voting on state budgets, taxes, and all state law.
contests.race_blurb: Both candidates advance to the general regardless of the primary result. An appointed Democratic incumbent with broad establishment endorsements faces a Republican retired CPA running an almost entirely fiscal platform.

candidates.summary: Appointed to the House in January 2025 after serving on the Redmond City Council; Redmond native with a research and community-engagement background.
highlights: Says he secured childcare and healthcare investments and 'passed more laws than any legislator this year,' citing bills on affordable housing, gun-violence prevention, and immigrant protections [S1]
Former Redmond city councilmember and UW student body president; appointed to succeed Vandana Slatter [S1][S3]
Endorsed by Rep. DelBene, Gov. Ferguson, the WA State Labor Council, and teachers and nurses unions [S1][S2]

experience.basis: Appointed incumbent with about 1.5 years of legislative record plus prior city council service — a record case, but a short one.

spending.basis: Campaigns on secured investments in childcare, healthcare, housing, and education.

housing.basis: Cites bills on affordable housing (candidate's own characterization); public-investment framing.

immigration.basis: Cites passed bills on immigrant protections (candidate's own characterization).

climate.basis: Lists climate action among priorities; endorsed by Washington Conservation Action.

social.basis: Endorsed by Planned Parenthood Alliance; no direct statements in the dossier.

candidates.summary: Retired CPA and financial systems consultant with 40 years of auditing experience, making his first run for state office on a fiscal-accountability platform.
highlights: Pledges to reject new taxes: Washington 'has a spending problem, not an income problem' [S1][S2]
Pledges to spearhead fraud audits and make state spending and performance metrics publicly accessible [S1][S2]
Accepts only individual contributions — no PAC or union money [S1][S2]

experience.basis: First-time state candidate with no prior elected public office; runs on an outside auditor's profile.

taxes.basis: Pledges to reject new taxes, framing the state as having 'a spending problem, not an income problem.'

spending.basis: Platform centers fraud audits, efficiency, and public spending/performance transparency.

reform.basis: Pledges to accept only individual contributions — no PAC or union money.

contests.office_does: A State Representative serves in Washington's 98-member House, writing and voting on state laws and the operating, capital, and transportation budgets, including K-12 funding, the state tax structure, housing mandates, and public-safety statutes.
contests.race_blurb: Appointed incumbent Zach Hall (D) faces Republican police chief Michelle Bennett, Democrat Aimee Warmerdam running to Hall's left, and no-party candidate Topher Leritz in a district Democrats hold but Republicans view as contestable. Taxes and public safety are the most documented contrasts in the field.

candidates.summary: Zach Hall (D) is the appointed incumbent, a former six-year Issaquah City Councilmember and Eastside Fire & Rescue Board chair who passed two bipartisan bills in his first legislative session.
highlights: Sponsored and passed HB 2296 (utility-financed home energy-efficiency upgrades) and HB 2575 (streamlined environmental reporting) in his first session [S3]
Voted for the new millionaire-earner income tax enacted this year [S5]
Six years on the Issaquah City Council and chaired the Eastside Fire & Rescue Board before appointment to the House [S1]
Endorsed by Rep. Schrier, WA Labor Council, WA Education Association, and Eastside Firefighters per his pamphlet [S1]; his campaign site adds Gov. Ferguson [S2]
Largest war chest in the race: $44,039 across 86 contributions [S6]

taxes.basis: Voted for the new millionaire-earner income tax; the dossier's sharpest documented tax-policy data point for him.

spending.basis: Supports fully funding public schools and expanding childcare, healthcare, and behavioral-health services — an invest-and-expand agenda.

climate.basis: Environment & Energy vice chair who passed a clean-energy-efficiency financing bill and is endorsed by WA Conservation Action, while framing energy policy around lowering costs.

experience.basis: Incumbent with two passed bills, six years on city council, fire-board chairmanship, and prior legislative-staff work — a proven-record profile.

reform.basis: Runs with the field's broadest institutional endorsement slate (governor, labor, party figures) and largest war chest, working within existing party structures.

healthcare.basis: Supports expanding access to affordable healthcare and behavioral-health services within the existing system, not single-payer replacement.

social.basis: Endorsed by Planned Parenthood Alliance Advocates, indicating alignment with reproductive-rights positions.

candidates.summary: Michelle Bennett (R) is Mercer Island's police chief with a 35-year law-enforcement career including chief posts in Maple Valley, Sammamish, and Mercer Island, and holds a Doctorate in Education.
highlights: Opposes an income tax and calls the recent state budget "the largest tax increase in state history" [S1]
Says police "have been left without clear tools to protect victims and neighborhoods" and runs on her three decades as a police chief in the district [S1]
Pledges to ensure state spending delivers measurable results [S1]
Second-highest fundraising in the race: $21,352 across 81 contributions [S5]

taxes.basis: Opposes an income tax and criticizes the recent budget as the largest tax increase in state history — explicit in both pamphlet and news coverage.

spending.basis: Pledges to ensure state spending delivers measurable results, framing her candidacy around respect for taxpayers.

safety.basis: Says police lack clear tools to protect victims and neighborhoods; runs on a 35-year enforcement career with multiple chief posts.

experience.basis: Deep institutional record as a law-enforcement executive, but a first-time candidate with no legislative record — mixed on record vs. renewal.

candidates.summary: Aimee Warmerdam (D) is a first-time candidate and grassroots organizer with Seattle DSA and Whole Washington, running to the incumbent's left on a single-payer and social-housing program.
highlights: Supports single-payer universal healthcare via the Whole Washington initiative [S1]
Champions a $26 minimum wage, a 32-hour workweek, a millionaire's tax, and a payroll tax on large companies [S2]
Supports new social housing, multi-family zoning, and tiny-house villages for homeless residents [S1]
Refuses corporate PAC money; smallest verified fundraising among the Democrats at $3,679 [S4]
Opposes AI data-center expansion on energy and environmental grounds [S2]

taxes.basis: Explicitly backs a millionaire's tax, a payroll tax on large companies, and progressive taxation of the ultra-rich to fund her program.

spending.basis: Program of major public expansion: fully funded schools, free school meals, tuition-free community college, expanded transit, and a state bank.

housing.basis: Centers social housing, anti-speculation measures, and tiny-house villages, while also supporting streamlined permitting and multi-family zoning.

climate.basis: Would reverse cuts to the Department of Natural Resources and opposes AI data-center expansion on energy and environmental grounds.

experience.basis: First-time legislative candidate with no office beyond PCO, running on organizing background and lived working-class experience.

reform.basis: Refuses corporate PAC money and runs against the "corporate duopoly," with minimal institutional fundraising.

healthcare.basis: Supports single-payer universal healthcare via the Whole Washington initiative — the only such candidate in the race.

tech.basis: Opposes AI data-center expansion on energy and environmental grounds, a limits-oriented posture toward the industry.

candidates.summary: Topher Leritz states no party preference and runs an explicitly nonpartisan campaign, accepting no donations or endorsements; his background is in marketing, political consulting, and private investigation.
highlights: Accepts zero endorsements or special-interest donations as a matter of principle [S1]
Criticizes current leadership for focusing on "reelection campaigns, political outrage, and national talking points" over local problems [S1]
Emphasizes affordable housing, public safety paired with mental-health services, and accountability [S2]

spending.basis: Emphasizes accountability and practical results from government rather than program expansion.

safety.basis: Pairs improved public-safety services with mental-health services, without leaning toward enforcement or prevention poles.

experience.basis: No elected experience; self-described "not a career politician" running as an outsider to both parties.

reform.basis: Refuses all donations and endorsements — zero PDC-reported contributions — making anti-special-interest practice his core differentiator.

contests.office_does: A State Representative writes and votes on Washington state law and the operating, capital, and transportation budgets, covering school funding, tax structure, housing mandates, health funding, and criminal-justice statutes.
contests.race_blurb: Nine-year incumbent Democrat Shelley Kloba faces an intra-party challenge from Bothell Deputy Mayor Jenne Alderks, who cites the 1st LD Democrats' sole endorsement, while Libertarian Jeff Lyon and Republican Cliff Moon contest the right-of-center lane with low-resource campaigns. The top two vote-getters advance to the general election regardless of party.

candidates.summary: Incumbent State Representative since 2017, Vice-Chair of the House Technology, Economic Development, and Veterans Committee, with a legislative focus on data privacy, AI consumer protection, and gambling and cannabis regulation.
highlights: Incumbent since 2017; Vice-Chair of the House Technology, Economic Development, and Veterans Committee and founder of the House Technology caucus [S1]
Sponsor of the People's Privacy Act and passed 2019 legislation creating a statewide gambling self-exclusion program [S3]
Says she will fix the regressive tax code and close tax loopholes, redirecting the revenue to schools [S2]
Endorsed by the WA State Labor Council, Sierra Club, WEA PAC, and Planned Parenthood Alliance Advocates [S1], plus House Speaker Laurie Jinkins per her campaign site [S2]
Leads the field in reported fundraising with $38.5k across 88 contributions [S4]

taxes.basis: Repeatedly pledges to fix the regressive tax code and close loopholes to redirect revenue to schools, without proposing broader new wealth taxes.

spending.basis: Platform emphasizes fully funding K-12, renewable-energy investment, and expanded behavioral-health access rather than audits or restraint.

safety.basis: Frames public safety around increasing access to behavioral health care rather than enforcement measures.

climate.basis: Supports green-jobs creation, renewable-energy investment, and waterway monitoring, and carries a Sierra Club endorsement.

experience.basis: Nine-plus years in the House with passed legislation, committee leadership, and endorsements including the House Speaker — the maximal proven-record profile in this field.

reform.basis: Campaigns on institutional coalition support — labor, party organizations, and elected officials including the House Speaker — rather than structural political reform.

healthcare.basis: Emphasizes increasing access to behavioral health care within existing structures; no universal-coverage proposal.

social.basis: Pledges to protect civil rights and is endorsed by Planned Parenthood Alliance Advocates and the Alliance for Gun Responsibility.

tech.basis: Legislative record centers on data privacy regulation: sponsor of the People's Privacy Act, reining in data brokers, and reducing AI harms to consumers.

candidates.summary: Bothell Deputy Mayor and human services specialist in behavioral health, mounting an intra-party challenge to the Democratic incumbent on a platform of affordability, universal healthcare, and her municipal record.
highlights: Bothell Deputy Mayor, first elected to the City Council in 2021 and re-elected in 2025; former Planning Commission member [S1]
Supports guaranteeing universal healthcare coverage [S1]
Cites her Bothell record on crisis-response programs and community court public-safety approaches [S2]
States she received the 1st LD Democrats' sole endorsement over the incumbent [S2]

spending.basis: Platform centers on expanding housing opportunities, transit investment, and universal healthcare rather than audits or restraint.

housing.basis: Supports expanding housing opportunities and serves on the board of a homelessness-services nonprofit; specific mechanism (supply vs. tenant protection) not stated.

safety.basis: Cites crisis-response programs and community court alternatives from her Bothell record as her public-safety approach.

experience.basis: Challenger to a nine-year incumbent offering municipal-executive and human-services experience rather than a legislative record.

healthcare.basis: Explicitly supports guaranteeing universal healthcare coverage in both her pamphlet statement and campaign site.

social.basis: Emphasizes protecting the rights, freedoms, and human dignity of all Washingtonians; former LGBTQIA+ youth group leader.

candidates.summary: Libertarian candidate from Woodinville, a disabled Navy veteran, former deputy sheriff, and cybersecurity engineering manager running on tax elimination for households under $125,000 and direct-democracy mechanisms.
highlights: Proposes eliminating state taxes for most families earning under $125,000 and requiring direct voter approval for any income tax [S1]
Opposes top-down state zoning mandates and supports reforming the Growth Management Act to increase housing supply [S2]
Disabled Navy veteran, former Chesapeake, Virginia deputy sheriff, and cybersecurity engineering manager [S1]
Supports binding citizen initiatives and decriminalizing victimless conduct while holding offenders accountable [S2]

taxes.basis: Platform centers on eliminating state taxes for most sub-$125k households and requiring voter approval for any income tax.

housing.basis: Would eliminate regulations he says drive up housing costs and reform the GMA to increase market supply.

safety.basis: Genuinely mixed: pairs decriminalizing victimless conduct with holding offenders accountable, citing his deputy-sheriff experience.

climate.basis: Mixed posture: supports advanced nuclear and expanded 'cleanest, most efficient' power while also proposing closed-loop cooling requirements for data centers; no stated position on carbon pricing.

experience.basis: No prior elected office and describes himself as proudly unaffiliated with the political establishment — the maximal outsider case.

local-control.basis: Explicitly opposes top-down state zoning mandates and argues communities should not be taxed for projects that do not benefit them.

reform.basis: Supports binding citizen initiatives immune to legal or bureaucratic reversal and runs an anti-establishment, near-zero-fundraising campaign.

tech.basis: Would require closed-loop cooling for data centers to reduce freshwater use — a targeted regulation, though he is otherwise a tech-industry executive promoting AI career pathways.

candidates.summary: Republican candidate with decades in the construction trades and small-business ownership, running on property-tax relief, full funding for local police, and reduced business regulation.
highlights: Centers property-tax relief, saying layered taxes drive up the cost of gas, groceries, and utilities [S1]
Supports law enforcement, full funding for local police, and criminal-justice accountability [S1]
Reports 63 years in the construction trades and 54 years of small-business ownership; endorsed by the King County Republican Party [S1]

taxes.basis: Platform centers on property-tax relief and argues layered taxes drive up the cost of gas, groceries, and utilities.

safety.basis: Runs on supporting law enforcement, fully funding local police, and criminal-justice accountability.

experience.basis: No elected experience beyond Precinct Committee Officer; offers decades of construction-trades and small-business background instead of government service.

social.basis: Says schools should focus on academics and parents should raise their children — a parental-rights framing.

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 11th Legislative District (Renton, Tukwila, and parts of south Seattle/south King County), serving a two-year term in the Washington State House.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Steve Bergquist is the only candidate who filed, so he advances from the primary automatically and faces no listed opponent.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state representative and active Renton high-school teacher, one of the only classroom teachers serving in the legislature; running unopposed.
highlights: Serves on the House Education and Appropriations committees while teaching at Lindbergh High School in Renton [S1][S2]
Cites passage of HB 1587 college promise scholarships as a signature accomplishment [S1]
Wants to expand childcare, preschool (Ready K), and college affordability programs, and to help create more housing near jobs [S1]

experience.basis: Long-serving incumbent with education-focused committee assignments and a cited legislative record (HB 1587).

spending.basis: Platform centers on expanding public programs: childcare, preschool, and college promise scholarships.

contests.office_does: One of two House members elected from the district to write state law and adopt Washington's operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Five-term Republican incumbent Mike Steele faces a well-funded intra-party challenge from Wenatchee pastor Adam James, who has out-raised him roughly four-to-one, while Democrat Maggie Adams of Snoqualmie is the only candidate from the district's King County side. In this cross-Cascades district a Republican-vs-Republican general election is possible under the top-two primary.

candidates.summary: Five-term incumbent Republican from Chelan, chamber-of-commerce director, and nine-year ranking member of the House Capital Budget Committee who runs on a bipartisan-dealmaker record.
highlights: Says the state has a spending problem, not a revenue problem; favors lower taxes, less red tape, and balanced budgets [S2][S4]
Ranking member of the House Capital Budget Committee for nine years; cites over $32 million secured for district projects in the 2026 supplemental capital budget [S3][S4]
Opposes decriminalization and emphasizes personal responsibility for offenders alongside improving officer-community relationships [S4]
Endorsed by the Washington State Labor Council, the Washington State Medical Association, and the House Republican Organizing Committee [S4]
Wants to move K-12 toward hands-on, competency-based learning and expanded career/technical training [S2][S4]

taxes.basis: Repeatedly campaigns on lower taxes, cutting B&O tax burdens, and the view that the state has a spending problem, not a revenue problem.

spending.basis: Explicitly frames the budget as a spending problem and favors balanced budgets protecting taxpayers before new spending.

safety.basis: Opposes decriminalization and stresses offender accountability, but pairs it with improving officer-community relationships rather than a maximal enforcement platform.

experience.basis: Five-term incumbent, past Deputy Minority Leader, nine years as Capital Budget ranking member with an enacted record.

healthcare.basis: Wants to expand affordable options via price transparency and cost control within existing market structures; no public-system transformation agenda.

candidates.summary: Wenatchee pastor and private Christian school co-founder with no prior elected office, challenging the incumbent from the right and leading the race in fundraising.
highlights: Platform of tax cuts, deregulation, and reducing state spending via government-efficiency measures [S2][S3]
Backs voter ID, proof of citizenship, and reform of the mail-in ballot system [S2][S3]
Runs as 'tough on crime,' opposing catch-and-release approaches and sanctuary-state policies [S2][S3]
Supports expanded school choice, 'parental empowerment,' and defense of traditional family structures, framing his candidacy as 'bold, Christian, and conservative leadership' [S2][S3]
Endorsed by the King, Snohomish, and Douglas county Republican Party organizations against the sitting Republican; dominant fundraiser at $343,869 [S1][S3][S6]

taxes.basis: Economy plank centers on tax cuts and deregulation.

spending.basis: Wants to reduce state spending through government-efficiency measures.

housing.basis: Prescribes permitting reform and easing requirements to lower home prices, with incentives for $200,000-or-less homes; a supply/deregulation approach.

safety.basis: Explicitly 'tough on crime,' opposes catch-and-release, backs law enforcement, and wants a different approach to homelessness and addiction.

experience.basis: No prior elected experience; runs as a movement-conservative outsider against a ten-year incumbent.

immigration.basis: Opposes sanctuary-state policies as part of his enforcement plank.

social.basis: School choice, 'parental empowerment,' opposition to 'ideological indoctrination,' and defense of traditional family structures are central campaign themes.

candidates.summary: Snoqualmie-based IATSE union business representative and professional stage worker, the only Democrat in the race and the only candidate from the district's King County side.
highlights: Supports a millionaire's tax and higher taxes on high-grossing businesses to fund tax fairness and inflation relief [S1][S3]
Views accessible, comprehensive healthcare as a right and wants to expand medical access across all regions of the state [S2][S3]
Would regulate vacation rentals and mandate affordable units in new construction [S3]
Emphasizes water conservation against data-center and energy-company demands, and opposes natural gas fracking threats to water [S2][S3]
Backs constitutional term limits and lowering Washington's voting age to 16 for state and local elections [S2]

taxes.basis: Supports a millionaire's tax and higher taxes on high-grossing businesses, with tax fairness as a platform plank.

spending.basis: Platform centers on expanded public investment: fully funding schools, raising teacher wages, and expanding healthcare access statewide.

housing.basis: Would regulate vacation rentals and mandate affordable units in new construction; regulatory rather than supply-side, but no social-housing or rent-stabilization agenda stated.

safety.basis: Emphasizes community policing and de-escalation rather than enforcement-first approaches.

climate.basis: Opposes natural gas fracking and prioritizes water/resource protection against energy-industry demands; no carbon-pricing position stated.

experience.basis: No prior elected government office; offers union bargaining leadership and lived-experience credentials rather than legislative tenure.

reform.basis: Supports constitutional term limits for elected offices and the federal judiciary plus lowering the voting age to 16 — structural-reform proposals no opponent raises.

healthcare.basis: Treats healthcare as a right and wants expanded access statewide, but does not explicitly call for single-payer.

tech.basis: Concerned that data centers' water and energy demands could crowd out community and agricultural needs.

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 30th Legislative District, serving a two-year term writing and voting on state law and the operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Kristine Reeves, chair of the House Agriculture and Natural Resources Committee, faces Paul McDaniel, a former appointed Federal Way City Councilmember who prefers the Independent Party. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state representative (2017-2019 and 2023-present) and Federal Way small business owner who chairs the House Agriculture and Natural Resources Committee.
highlights: Says she passed laws protecting homeless students, expanding senior food access, mitigating wildfire risk, and investing $100 million in local public safety [S1]
Founded the bipartisan Veterans & Military Families Caucus and passed the Wounded Warrior Shared Leave Act [S1]
Endorsed by Congressman Adam Smith, labor, and environmental groups including Sierra Club Washington and Transportation for Washington [S1][S3][S4]

experience.basis: Multi-term incumbent with committee chairmanship and a cited record of passed legislation; opponent has no legislative experience.

spending.basis: Runs on program delivery: $100 million public-safety investment, senior food access, and protections for homeless students.

climate.basis: Cites wildfire-risk-mitigation legislation and holds Sierra Club Washington's 2026 primary endorsement.

candidates.summary: Former appointed Federal Way City Councilmember, Marine Corps Iraq War veteran, and technology-consulting business owner running with an Independent Party preference.
highlights: Says rising housing, food, gas, and energy costs are hurting working families and pledges to reduce taxes [S1]
Frames Olympia as marked by "fraud, corruption, and weak leadership" and stresses better management of existing government resources [S1][S2]
Would expand affordable housing by removing barriers to "middle housing," empower law enforcement, and "protect girls' sports" [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Pledges to reduce taxes, framing rising costs and "Olympia demanding more" as the problem.

spending.basis: Alleges fraud and weak leadership in Olympia and stresses managing existing resources rather than new spending.

housing.basis: Housing approach is removing barriers to "middle housing" — a supply-side mechanism — alongside a general affordable-housing goal.

safety.basis: Says he would empower law enforcement with necessary tools; no prevention-side proposals documented.

social.basis: Single stated plank to "protect girls' sports," a traditional-values position on gender policy.

experience.basis: Outsider change message against a multi-term incumbent, though he holds some elected experience via an appointed council seat.

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 31st Legislative District, serving a two-year term voting on state law and the operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Republican incumbent Joshua Penner, a first-term representative and former eight-year Orting mayor, faces Democrat John Bielka, a first-time candidate and career civil engineer/public works director. Both emphasize affordability and accountability; both advance to the November general election.

candidates.summary: Democratic first-time candidate, a civil engineer and public works director with more than 30 years managing infrastructure, budgets, and operations, who raises cattle outside Orting.
highlights: Pledges to "work across the aisle to lower everyday costs" with responsible budgeting and reduced unnecessary regulation [S1][S2]
Emphasizes faster delivery of transportation, housing, and flood-protection projects and expanded career and technical education [S1][S2]
Website states support for the Second Amendment and for adequate police staffing; campaign tagline is "Results, not rhetoric" [S2]

experience.basis: Self-described "first time public servant" with deep professional but no elected or legislative experience.

spending.basis: Emphasizes responsible budgeting, "transparent math," bill-reading, and taxpayer protection — an accountability-first framing.

safety.basis: Supports adequate police staffing; no prevention-side proposals documented.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Republican first-term state representative, an eight-year former mayor of Orting and Marine Corps Iraq War veteran serving as Assistant Ranking Member on Appropriations.
highlights: Pamphlet cites saving Buckley's Rainier School, preventing early-education cuts, and cutting red tape to fund White River and Fairfax bridge repairs [S1]
Led efforts to modify the "Keeping Families Together Act" and challenged Sound Transit-related legislation (SB 6309) on zoning bypass and financial-accountability grounds [S2]
Fourteen years of municipal elected service (mayor and councilmember) before the legislature [S1]

experience.basis: Fourteen years of municipal elected office plus one legislative term with cited accomplishments — a record profile, though short legislative tenure.

spending.basis: Brand is oversight and "real accountability" (agency oversight, Sound Transit financial scrutiny), though he also defended specific programs from cuts.

local-control.basis: Challenged Sound Transit-related legislation (SB 6309) on grounds it bypassed local zoning.

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 32nd Legislative District (Shoreline, Lynnwood, Mountlake Terrace, Edmonds area), serving a two-year term voting on state law and budgets.
contests.race_blurb: An intra-Democratic contest: incumbent Lauren Davis, known for her behavioral-health legislative record, is challenged by Imraan Siddiqi, Executive Director of CAIR-Washington, who runs with a progressive-movement coalition. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state representative since 2019 whose career centers on behavioral health, including founding the Washington Recovery Alliance and championing Ricky's Law.
highlights: Passed Ricky's Law (involuntary crisis commitment for life-threatening addiction) and created the Recovery Navigator Program, plus treatment programs inside jails and ERs [S1][S2][S3]
Has "championed healthcare for immigrants and protections against ICE" and describes herself as "completely pro-choice" [S1]
Endorsed by Governors Ferguson and Gregoire, Representatives DelBene, Larsen, and Smith, the State Labor Council, and a broad union slate [S1]

experience.basis: Seven-year incumbent with one of the Legislature's most identifiable single-issue records (Ricky's Law, Recovery Navigator Program).

safety.basis: Treatment-and-recovery-led record (Recovery Navigator, jail/ER treatment, crisis care center), tempered by the involuntary-commitment mechanism of Ricky's Law.

immigration.basis: States she has championed healthcare for immigrants and protections against ICE.

social.basis: Describes herself as "completely pro-choice."

spending.basis: Record is built on funding and expanding programs: treatment access, domestic-violence victim services, and the Lynnwood Crisis Care Center.

candidates.summary: First-time Democratic candidate and Executive Director of CAIR-Washington, a civil rights advocate and former small business owner challenging the incumbent with a progressive-movement coalition.
highlights: Core planks are protecting immigrant communities facing "federal targeting" and building "a Washington that stands up to federal overreach" [S1][S2]
One of the original plaintiffs against the Trump administration's "Muslim Ban"; organizes coalitions with labor unions and tenant-rights advocates [S1]
Reported endorsements include U.S. Reps. Rashida Tlaib and Ilhan Omar, 32nd LD seatmate Rep. Cindy Ryu, UFCW 3000 (dual), and the Transit Riders Union [S3]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record, explicitly framing the race as current leaders not doing enough.

immigration.basis: CAIR-Washington executive director, Muslim Ban plaintiff, and a platform centered on protecting immigrants from federal targeting.

housing.basis: Pledges to fight for housing affordability and organizes coalitions with tenant-rights advocates.

contests.office_does: One of two House members elected from the district to write state law and adopt Washington's operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Twelve-year Democratic incumbent Mia Gregerson, Vice-Chair of House Appropriations, faces a same-party challenge from Burien City Councilmember Alex Andrade, who stresses spending accountability, plus Republican first-time candidate Yuri Marinchik running an anti-career-politician campaign.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democrat since 2013, former SeaTac mayor, and Vice-Chair of House Appropriations who runs on delivered programs like free school meals and the Right to Repair law.
highlights: Sponsored HB 1483 (2025), Washington's Right to Repair Act, and HB 1404 requiring free school breakfast and lunch statewide from 2026-27 [S3][S4]
Points to state programs as the affordability answer: school meals, emergency food assistance, and free transit for kids [S1]
Vice-Chair of House Appropriations and former SeaTac mayor when the city passed the nation's first $15 minimum wage [S1]
Official legislative page highlights 2025-26 bills described as strengthening community safety and protecting immigrants [S2]
Endorsed by the Washington State Labor Council, OneAmerica Votes, Sierra Club Washington, and Gov. Ferguson [S1][S6]

spending.basis: Runs on expanding and delivering state programs — universal free school meals, food assistance, free youth transit, corridor funding — as the primary affordability lever.

climate.basis: Endorsed by Sierra Club Washington in this primary; no specific climate-policy statements in the dossier.

experience.basis: Representative since 2013 with a budget-writing leadership post, a sponsored-bill record, and prior service as SeaTac mayor.

immigration.basis: Official page highlights bills protecting immigrants, and she carries the OneAmerica Votes endorsement.

social.basis: Says she helped establish Washington's first Office of Equity.

tech.basis: Sponsored and campaigns on the Right to Repair law restricting manufacturers' parts-pairing practices — consumer-side tech regulation.

candidates.summary: Burien City Councilmember elected in 2024 and small business owner, challenging the 12-year Democratic incumbent on spending accountability and public safety.
highlights: Says state spending 'continues to grow without enough accountability' and pledges transparency in how taxpayer dollars are spent [S1]
Supports co-response teams pairing first responders with behavioral health professionals, and 'responsible policies to address large unmanaged encampments' [S2]
Opposes unfunded state mandates on cities, arguing cities should make decisions reflecting their unique needs [S2]
Supports pro-choice protections and equality [S1]
Endorsement base is South King County city officials rather than state figures, labor, or party organizations [S2]

spending.basis: Central plank is that state spending grows without enough accountability, demanding transparency — an accountability lane, not an anti-spending one.

safety.basis: Mixed: backs behavioral-health co-response teams while calling for accountability-focused policies on large unmanaged encampments.

experience.basis: First-term councilmember (2024) and small business owner running on local-government freshness against a 12-year incumbent.

local-control.basis: Explicitly opposes unfunded state mandates on cities and argues for city-level decision-making.

social.basis: Pamphlet lists support for pro-choice protections and equality.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate, a working-class immigrant building a small business with his wife, running against 'career politicians' on cost of living.
highlights: Says he will fight for lower housing and energy costs and 'fair taxes' [S1]
Criticizes 'endless rules that make life harder and increase government control' [S1]
Signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge supporting a congressional term-limits convention [S4]
Minimal fundraising (~$1,500) and no endorsements found beyond the term-limits organization [S3][S4]

spending.basis: Frames government rules and control as the problem itself; a restraint orientation without specific budget proposals.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected or governmental record, running explicitly against career politicians.

reform.basis: Signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge — a documented structural-reform commitment.

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 34th District (West Seattle, Vashon Island, White Center, west Burien), serving two-year terms writing state law and budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Fifteen-year incumbent and House Majority Leader Joe Fitzgibbon faces political newcomer Mary Anito; both prefer the Democratic Party and both advance to the general. The visible contrasts are experience (legislative leadership vs. private-sector operations) and fiscal emphasis, with a notable evidence asymmetry since Anito's platform detail is limited.

candidates.summary: State Representative since 2010 and House Majority Leader since 2022, with nine years chairing the House Environment & Energy Committee and a signature record on climate legislation and the 2026 tax-reform package.
highlights: Championed the Climate Commitment Act and Clean Fuel Standard; pamphlet describes passing 'the nation's strongest climate laws' [S1][S3]
Says he led passage of the 'millionaires' tax' paired with tax cuts for working families and small businesses [S1]
House Majority Leader since 2022 after chairing Environment & Energy from 2014 to 2022; now also on Appropriations and Rules [S1][S3]

taxes.basis: Says he led passage of the millionaires-tax reform package — an enacted progressive-revenue record.

spending.basis: Pamphlet emphasizes securing investments in education, healthcare, transit, public safety, and the environment, alongside budget stabilization.

housing.basis: Supports both supply-side measures (building near transit, modernizing local planning) and public-investment measures (affordable housing, homelessness prevention, limiting displacement).

climate.basis: Championed the Climate Commitment Act and Clean Fuel Standard and pledges to continue advancing clean energy and emission reductions.

experience.basis: Fifteen-year incumbent and House Majority Leader — among the most senior legislators on the King County ballot.

local-control.basis: Supports 'modernizing local planning' and served on the board of Futurewise, a growth-management advocacy organization — an indirect lean toward state/regional standards.

social.basis: Defends reproductive freedom, voting rights, and racial and environmental justice in both pamphlet and campaign materials.

candidates.summary: First-time candidate with an operations and supply-chain management background at REI, Wayfair, Caterpillar, and Cisco, running on pairing fiscal responsibility with public investment.
highlights: Argues 'fiscal responsibility and strong public investment are not mutually exclusive'; wants to balance the budget while funding infrastructure, education, and healthcare [S1]
Operations and engineering background (MIT Leaders for Global Operations Fellow; senior operations manager at REI) with board service at ROOTS Young Adult Shelter [S1]
Political newcomer challenging the House Majority Leader; platform detail beyond the pamphlet was not retrievable [S1][S4]

spending.basis: Explicitly pairs balancing the budget with funding infrastructure, education, and healthcare — a stated mix of restraint and investment.

experience.basis: First-time candidate and political newcomer running on private-sector operations experience rather than any governmental record.

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 36th District (Ballard, Magnolia, Queen Anne, Greenwood area of northwest Seattle), serving two-year terms writing state law and budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Liz Berry, House Labor Committee chair and prime sponsor of the 2025 permit-to-purchase firearm law, is the only candidate filed and advances unopposed on the primary ballot.

candidates.summary: Third-term incumbent State Representative serving since 2021 and chair of the House Labor Committee, with a signature record on gun-safety legislation and a career in reproductive-rights and consumer advocacy.
highlights: Prime sponsor of HB 1163 (2025), Washington's permit-to-purchase firearm law, signed by Gov. Ferguson in May 2025 [S3]
Chairs the House Labor Committee; cites legislation protecting workers from wage theft and unfair noncompete agreements [S1]
Former Legislative Director for Congresswoman Gabby Giffords and past Pro-Choice Washington board member; has worked to protect and expand abortion access [S1]

taxes.basis: Supported tax cuts on essentials while 'making our tax system fairer' to fund services, and calls the 2026 tax-code rebalancing package 'a historic step.'

experience.basis: Third-term incumbent with committee chairmanship and a concrete legislative signature (HB 1163), though tenure is moderate.

social.basis: Lifelong reproductive-justice advocate (past Pro-Choice Washington board) with legislative work to protect and expand abortion access, plus signature gun-safety law.

spending.basis: Pamphlet explicitly frames a fairer tax system as the means 'to fund schools, childcare, housing, and health care' — the same invest-and-expand plank that earned +1 spending scores for comparable candidates (Orwall, Alvarado, Fitzgibbon) in this batch. Omission is inconsistent.

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 37th District (southeast Seattle, Skyway, part of Renton), voting on state law, budget, and taxes.
contests.race_blurb: An open seat vacated by Rep. Chipalo Street's Senate run. Nonprofit executive Jaelynn Scott (D), heavily endorsed by Democratic officials, faces first-time Independent candidate Evon McCorkle, a college student with pamphlet-only evidence; both advance to the general.

candidates.summary: Executive Director of the Lavender Rights Project and community organizer with an extensive endorsement slate; would be the first openly transgender person to serve in the Washington Legislature.
highlights: Says she built a coalition that redirected $16 million from jail expansion into supportive housing and alternative public-safety approaches [S1][S2]

Platform includes 'compassionate public safety' (behavioral-health responders over police), universal healthcare, universal childcare, and progressive taxation on wealthy residents and corporations [S1][S2]
Endorsements span Rep. Pramila Jayapal, Sen. Bernie Sanders, King County Executive Girmay Zahilay, and numerous local Democratic officials [S1][S4]

taxes.basis: Calls for progressive taxation on wealthy residents and corporations and expanded cash assistance like the Working Families Tax Credit.

spending.basis: Platform of universal childcare, publicly funded preschool, and expanded cash assistance treats public investment as the primary lever.

housing.basis: Wants to expand affordable and permanent supportive housing on the Chief Seattle Club model, with a record of redirecting jail funds into supportive housing.

safety.basis: 'Compassionate public safety': behavioral-health professionals instead of police for crises, violence interruption funding, police accountability, and the $16M jail-to-housing redirection.

experience.basis: No prior elected office — an outsider by tenure — though with nonprofit executive leadership and a concrete claimed policy outcome.

immigration.basis: Supports limits on surveillance technology and data sharing with federal agencies.

healthcare.basis: Supports universal healthcare including vision, dental, and mental health coverage.

social.basis: Leads a nonprofit for Black gender-diverse communities and platforms protecting Medicaid, reproductive, and gender-affirming care.

tech.basis: Supports limits on surveillance technology and data sharing — a regulatory posture toward tech.

candidates.summary: College student studying mathematics and physics and first-time candidate preferring the Independent Party; a lifelong district resident running on education access, housing stability, and restoring trust in government.
highlights: First-time candidate with no prior elected experience; states he is 'not backed by powerful insiders or political machines' [S1]
Lead priorities are education access, affordable housing and housing stability, and community safety [S1]
Evidence rests on the pamphlet alone: his campaign website was unreachable and no substantive news coverage was found [S1]

experience.basis: College student and first-time candidate with no prior elected experience, running as an outsider.

reform.basis: Frames his candidacy around restoring trust between residents and officials and being 'not backed by powerful insiders or political machines.'

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for the 41st District (Bellevue, Mercer Island, Newcastle, parts of Renton, Issaquah, and Sammamish), voting on state law, budget, and taxes.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat and House Deputy Majority Leader My-Linh Thai faces first-time Independent candidate Michael Rosen, an attorney and investor running on fiscal accountability; both advance to the general. The clearest divide is fiscal: protecting service investments versus spending restraint.

candidates.summary: Incumbent State Representative since 2019 and House Deputy Majority Leader; a pharmacist and former Bellevue School Board President who describes herself as the first refugee elected to the Washington Legislature.
highlights: House Deputy Majority Leader with seats on eight committees including Appropriations and Finance [S1]
Top stated priorities are protecting access to affordable, high-quality healthcare and expanding behavioral health services [S1]
Former Bellevue School District Board President and Vice-President of the Washington State School Directors' Association [S1]

spending.basis: Emphasizes protecting care access and expanding behavioral health and education investment rather than spending restraint.

climate.basis: States support for 'meaningful action on climate change' and scientific innovation.

experience.basis: Seven-year incumbent with a House leadership post, broad committee assignments, and prior school-board leadership — a sustained governmental record.

healthcare.basis: Healthcare priorities framed as protecting access to affordable care and expanding behavioral health within existing structures; no universal-system platform.

candidates.summary: Attorney, investor, and licensed nutritionist from Mercer Island; a first-time candidate preferring the Independent Party who is running on fiscal discipline and accountability for state spending.
highlights: Argues state spending 'more than doubled in two decades' while accountability, affordability, public safety, and health outcomes worsened; calls for fiscal discipline [S1]
Fourth-generation Washingtonian with a private-sector legal, investment, and nutrition background and no prior elected experience [S1][S3]
Describes himself as thinking independently and valuing 'outcomes over ideology'; his campaign site had no substantive policy content, so evidence rests on the pamphlet [S1][S2]

spending.basis: Core of his platform: state spending doubled while outcomes worsened; demands fiscal discipline and accountable treatment of tax dollars.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no prior elected experience, running on a private-sector background and independent thinking.

contests.office_does: One of two House seats for LD 43 (central Seattle); votes on state law, the state budget, and taxes.
contests.race_blurb: Single-candidate race: first-term incumbent Rep. Shaun Scott is the only candidate on the primary ballot and advances to the general automatically.

candidates.summary: First-term incumbent elected in 2024, Vice Chair of the House Labor & Workplace Standards Committee, and the first open socialist in the Washington Legislature since the 1910s.
highlights: Credits corporate tax-break repeal work with preserving $645 million in biennial spending on housing, wildfire mitigation, and schools [S1]
Passed a bill requiring large financial institutions to pay standard document recording fees, funding affordable housing [S3]
Seattle DSA member; Vice Chair of the House Labor & Workplace Standards Committee [S1][S3]

taxes.basis: Record of ending corporate tax breaks to preserve $645 million in spending on public services.

spending.basis: Record and framing center on preserving and expanding public investment in housing, schools, and higher education.

housing.basis: Passed document recording fee parity bill generating tens of millions annually for affordable housing.

climate.basis: Sponsored wildfire response funding; preserved wildfire-mitigation spending; endorsed by Washington Conservation Action.

experience.basis: First-term incumbent with passed bills and a committee vice-chairmanship, but a short legislative tenure.

reform.basis: Frames major corporate lobbying as an oligarchic threat to public services.

social.basis: Endorsed by Planned Parenthood Alliance Advocates across his two campaigns.

contests.office_does: A state representative serves a two-year term in Washington's 98-member House, voting on state budgets, taxes, and laws covering schools, housing, healthcare, transportation, climate, and civil rights.
contests.race_blurb: An open seat after longtime Rep. Larry Springer's retirement, with two Democrats — Redmond City Council President Vanessa Kritzer and technology attorney Chandler Torbett — treated as the serious contenders; Republican John P Gibbons filed no statement or fundraising.

candidates.summary: Redmond City Councilmember (2020–present) and Council President, and Executive Director of the Washington Association of Land Trusts, running on her municipal housing, tenant-protection, and climate record.
highlights: Six-plus years on the Redmond City Council, including as Council President; strengthened inclusionary zoning and led creation of a renter-protections code chapter [S4]
Supports progressive revenue including recent high-earner taxes and is open to broader structural reform such as an income tax [S4]
Record includes Redmond's climate emergency declaration, environmental action plan, and salmon recovery leadership [S4]; leads with climate, housing, and childcare [S1]
Holds the 45th District Democrats, WEA, State Labor Council, and most area electeds [S1]; leads fundraising with about $109K reported [S3]
Backed a Redmond resolution funding organizations serving immigrants and refugees; wants state anti-discrimination and healthcare-access backstops [S4]

taxes.basis: Calls the tax code deeply regressive, supports high-earner taxes, and is open to structural income-tax reform and head-tax-style tools.

spending.basis: Platform centers public investment in housing, childcare, and climate; Seattle Times criticized her for naming no disagreement with her party's spending direction.

housing.basis: Led renter-protections code chapter, supports rent stabilization and permanent supportive housing, though paired with supply and zoning work.

climate.basis: Career conservation leadership plus a council record of climate emergency declaration, environmental action plan, and Climate Commitment Act funding.

experience.basis: The elected-record candidate in the race: six-plus years on the Redmond council with enacted housing and climate policy.

local-control.basis: Supports a stronger state role setting 'the floor' on housing production and calls affordability a regional/statewide problem.

immigration.basis: Backed a Redmond resolution affirming protections and funding for organizations serving immigrants and refugees.

social.basis: Pledges to defend civil rights with state anti-discrimination and healthcare-access backstops; endorsed by Planned Parenthood Alliance.

candidates.summary: A technology attorney and first-time candidate raised on the Muscogee Nation reservation, running on affordability, universal pre-K, AI consumer protections, and government accountability; endorsed by the Seattle Times.
highlights: Centers affordability — housing, childcare, groceries, insurance — and pledges urgency and execution over ideology [S4]
Supports the million-dollar earners income tax and capital gains tax but says new taxes should come with broad-based relief [S4]
The only candidate centering AI regulation: wants consumer-protection guardrails on AI in hiring, healthcare, and finance while encouraging innovation [S4]
Says the Legislature must 'prove we can use this money wisely' and is open to market-based solutions in some cases [S5]
Endorsed by the Seattle Times editorial board and by retiring Rep. Larry Springer (who endorsed both Democrats) [S5]

taxes.basis: Supports progressive revenue including the high-earner income tax and capital gains tax, but criticizes tax packages that lack broad-based relief.

spending.basis: Runs on accountability and delivery — 'We've got to prove we can use this money wisely' — while still backing new programs like universal pre-K.

housing.basis: Emphasizes supply through zoning reform and density near transit; no tenant-protection or public-housing platform in the dossier.

climate.basis: Wants to accelerate the clean-energy transition, primarily by removing permitting and administrative obstacles.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record, running on fresh execution and lived experience against a six-year councilmember.

tech.basis: Wants AI consumer-protection guardrails in hiring, healthcare, and financial decisions, balanced with encouraging innovation and jobs.

candidates.summary: The sole Republican-preference candidate in the race; he submitted no pamphlet statement, lists no website, and shows no reported fundraising.
highlights: Provided no statement, platform, biography, or website to the voters' pamphlet [S1]
No contributions for a 2026 campaign appear in PDC data [S2]
Likely the same John Gibbons who lost LD45 House general elections in 2020 and 2022 by roughly 45-point margins, though identity could not be conclusively verified [S3]

contests.office_does: One of two House members for LD 46 (northeast Seattle); writes and votes on state law and the state operating, capital, and transportation budgets.
contests.race_blurb: Both candidates advance to the general regardless of the primary result. A Democratic incumbent with passed bills and committee leadership faces a first-time independent whose core message is rejecting party-line politics.

candidates.summary: Incumbent state representative and disability-equity specialist with a legislative record on food access, caregiver workforce, and disability policy.
highlights: Passed HB 2294 banning covenants that block new grocery stores and pharmacies, and HB 1395 removing caregiver employment barriers [S3][S4]
First Middle Eastern woman elected to the Washington Legislature; Vice Chair of Civil Rights & Judiciary [S1][S2]
Endorsed by the 46th District Democrats, Pro-Choice Washington, WA State Labor Council, AG Nick Brown, and Rep. Pramila Jayapal [S1]

experience.basis: Incumbent with committee leadership roles and multiple bills passed, facing a first-time opponent.

social.basis: Endorsed by Pro-Choice Washington; civil-rights committee leadership and equity-focused career.

safety.basis: Board member of the Washington Coalition for Police Accountability; no direct policy statements in the dossier.

candidates.summary: First-time candidate preferring the 'Independent Party' whose central message is constituent responsiveness over party-line politics.
highlights: 35 years with the Lake Washington Education Association; long record in sports officiating and water-ski organizations [S1]
Supports 'Washington State citizenship for undocumented residents' and a three-year college path [S1]
Runs as an independent because 'Olympia needs a culture change'; calls for fiscal accountability before spending [S1][S2]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected experience, running against a sitting legislator.

reform.basis: Runs as an independent against party-line politics, arguing officials follow party leadership rather than constituents.

immigration.basis: Supports 'Washington State citizenship for undocumented residents.'

spending.basis: Calls for fiscal accountability and 'a government that listens to citizens before it spends their money.'

contests.office_does: One of two House members for LD 47 (Kent, Auburn, Covington, Maple Valley area); writes and votes on state law and the state budgets.
contests.race_blurb: A rematch of the 2024 general election: Democratic incumbent Chris Stearns, the House Speaker Pro Tempore, again faces Republican Ted Cooke, a party-organization activist and software professional without prior elected office. Both advance to the general.

candidates.summary: Incumbent state representative and House Speaker Pro Tempore, a Navajo attorney with roughly 30 years working on Native American rights and multiple bills signed into law.
highlights: Bills signed into law include HB 1461 (clean energy infrastructure), a tribal apprenticeship bill, and HB 1681 doubling problem-gambling funds [S3]
Describes reproductive and gender-affirming care as human rights [S1][S2]
Speaker Pro Tempore with prior Auburn City Council service and a Clinton-era federal appointment; broad labor and Democratic endorsement slate [S1][S2]

experience.basis: Incumbent with House leadership roles, bills signed into law, and prior council, commission, and federal service.

housing.basis: Cites expanding affordable housing through state programs as a delivered result and continued priority.

spending.basis: Runs on delivered state investments in affordable housing, behavioral health, childcare, and nutrition.

climate.basis: Sponsored HB 1461 on clean energy infrastructure, signed into law; supports clean-energy job expansion.

social.basis: Describes reproductive and gender-affirming care as human rights, repeated across pamphlet and campaign site.

safety.basis: Prioritizes behavioral-health investment; served on the King County Mental Illness and Drug Dependency Committee.

tech.basis: Sponsored HB 1221 on data privacy and security, which passed the Senate.

candidates.summary: Republican challenger and senior software tester with King County GOP organizational experience; he lost to Stearns in the 2024 general and runs on tax elimination, state audits, and parental rights.
highlights: Would freeze all new taxes, eliminate taxes on income, property, and fuel, and order audits of every state office and program [S1]
Would replace DEI with merit in government hiring, 'uphold the biological definition of gender,' and restore 'full parental rights in education' [S1][S2]
Prioritizes 'starter homes with yards' over high-density apartments he characterizes as forced on the district by Olympia [S1]

experience.basis: No prior elected public office; runs as an outsider against government overreach after losing to the same incumbent in 2024.

taxes.basis: Would freeze all new taxes and eliminate existing taxes on income, property, and fuel.

spending.basis: Would order audits of every state office and program, cut waste, and end commissions he characterizes as unaccountable.

housing.basis: Prioritizes market single-family 'starter homes with yards' and opposes state-driven density; no tenant-protection or public-housing agenda.

safety.basis: Names public safety a top priority and supports funding police to secure neighborhoods, parks, and schools.

social.basis: Would remove DEI from government hiring, 'uphold the biological definition of gender,' and restore 'full parental rights in education.'

local-control.basis: Characterizes density as forced on the district by Olympia and describes state government as overreaching in regulation and mandates.

contests.office_does: One of two House members for LD 48 (Redmond, Bellevue, Kirkland area); serves a two-year term voting on state budgets, taxes, and all state law.
contests.race_blurb: A Democrat-vs-Democrat contest: incumbent Rep. Amy Walen, a self-described pragmatic Democrat in the moderate/business lane, is challenged from the progressive/labor lane by Redmond City Councilmember Jessica Forsythe. Both advance to the general.

candidates.summary: Incumbent state representative since 2019, chair of the House Consumer Protection & Business Committee, and former Kirkland mayor who self-describes as a 'pragmatic governing Democrat.'
highlights: Says she voted no on 'the income tax' and champions balanced budgets and fiscal responsibility [S1]
Backs a 'balanced approach' to public safety: community-focused law enforcement plus expanded mental health and crisis response; endorsed by the Bellevue Police Guild [S1][S2]
Cites votes banning ICE from wearing masks and backfilling Planned Parenthood funding [S1]

experience.basis: House member since 2019 with a committee chairmanship, plus a decade in Kirkland city government including the mayoralty.

taxes.basis: Highlights her no vote on 'the income tax' (her framing of the capital gains tax) and tax relief for families and small businesses.

spending.basis: Emphasizes balanced budgets, fiscal responsibility, and combating wasteful spending, citing the regional homelessness authority.

housing.basis: Supports both housing investment and missing-middle supply reform; no tenant-protection emphasis — mixed on this axis.

safety.basis: Explicitly 'balanced approach': investment in community-focused law enforcement (Bellevue Police Guild endorsement) alongside expanded mental health and crisis response.

climate.basis: Supports clean energy and low-carbon fuel standards.

immigration.basis: Cites her vote to ban ICE from wearing masks.

social.basis: Prioritizes protecting reproductive healthcare access; cites backfilling Planned Parenthood funding.

candidates.summary: Redmond city councilmember since 2020 and past council president challenging the incumbent from the progressive/labor lane, with the local Democratic-organization and state labor council endorsements.
highlights: Cites winning tenant protections and securing $7.5M for housing and human services on the Redmond council [S1][S2]
Co-authored Redmond's Climate Emergency Declaration; chaired the council's Alternative Crisis Response Subcommittee [S1][S2]
Endorsed by the 48th District Democrats, King County Democrats, WA State Labor Council, and Sen. Vandana Slatter [S1]

experience.basis: Challenger with a municipal record (Redmond council since 2020) but no state legislative record, running against a 7-year incumbent.

housing.basis: Cites winning tenant protections and securing $7.5M for housing and human services; campaigns on renter protections.

climate.basis: Co-authored Redmond's Climate Emergency Declaration and its Environmental Sustainability Action Plan; campaigns on climate resiliency.

safety.basis: Chaired Redmond's Alternative Crisis Response Subcommittee.

spending.basis: Campaigns on equitable K-12 funding and public investment in housing, human services, and behavioral health.

social.basis: Campaigns on reproductive rights; leads Emerge WA training women and non-binary leaders; Planned Parenthood endorsement.

contests.office_does: One of two state representatives for the 5th Legislative District (Issaquah, Snoqualmie, North Bend, Maple Valley, Black Diamond, Enumclaw, parts of Sammamish), serving a two-year term in the Washington State House.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Lisa Callan, a House Capital Budget Committee vice-chair, faces Republican first-time candidate Patrick Peacock, a retired Army intelligence officer. With only two candidates, both advance to the November general election regardless of primary results.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate with 23 years of U.S. Army service as a Human Intelligence Warrant Officer, now a Senior Industrial Security Specialist at Boeing and Vice Chair of the King County GOP.
highlights: Opposes a state income tax and wants to roll back fuel taxes he says drive up costs [S1][S2]
Would reduce regulatory barriers to increase housing construction and lower costs [S1][S2]
Supports law enforcement and accountability for repeat offenders; frames the race as a check on "one-party rule" in Olympia [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Repeated explicit opposition to a state income tax and calls to roll back fuel taxes across pamphlet and campaign site.

climate.basis: Says high fuel taxes drive up costs and wants them rolled back — an energy-affordability-first posture, though he does not name the CCA.

housing.basis: Housing platform is entirely supply-side: reduce regulatory barriers to construction to lower costs, with no public-investment component.

safety.basis: Supports law enforcement and accountability for repeat offenders; no prevention or services component in his platform.

social.basis: Education plank calls for empowering parents and prioritizing reading, math, and practical skills.

experience.basis: First-time candidate who describes himself as "not a career politician"; military-intelligence career, no elected record.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state representative, Vice-Chair of the House Capital Budget Committee and a former Issaquah School Board director and Boeing lead engineer.
highlights: Holds budget-committee leadership: Capital Budget vice-chair plus Appropriations and K-12 Education committee seats [S1]
Priorities include public school funding, kids' mental health, workforce housing, affordable childcare, and a tax code that "doesn't squeeze working people" [S1]
Endorsed by educators, the State Labor Council, Sierra Club, Planned Parenthood, and the Alliance for Gun Responsibility [S1]

experience.basis: Multi-term incumbent with budget-committee leadership and prior school-board service, facing a first-time candidate.

taxes.basis: Wants a tax code that "doesn't squeeze working people" — a burden-shifting framing, though she names no specific new revenue source.

spending.basis: Platform leads with public investment: school funding, kids' mental health care, affordable childcare, and workforce-housing investment.

housing.basis: Emphasizes investment in "workforce housing for the families who serve our communities" rather than deregulation.

climate.basis: Pledges to protect forests and clean air; endorsed by Sierra Club and Washington Conservation Action.

social.basis: Explicitly pledges to defend reproductive freedom and supports gun-safety measures; endorsed by Planned Parenthood.

contests.office_does: The single state senator for the 30th Legislative District (Federal Way, Algona, Pacific, Milton area), serving a four-year term voting on state budgets and legislation and confirming gubernatorial appointments.
contests.race_blurb: Incumbent Democrat Claire Wilson, a 35-year educator with an early-learning-centered record, faces Republican first-time candidate Michael Rutland, a retired Air Force senior master sergeant and former Boeing manager. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Democratic state senator and 35-year educator who served on the Federal Way School Board 2011-2019; her legislative focus is early learning, childcare, and youth services.
highlights: States she sponsored and passed "landmark investments" in affordable childcare and early learning, and secured over $7.5 million for the city of Pacific after flooding [S1]
Voted for law-enforcement pay raises and additional training alongside funding for drug treatment and recovery [S1]
Endorsed by firefighters, Planned Parenthood, teachers, nurses, the Alliance for Gun Responsibility, and the State Labor Council [S1]

experience.basis: Incumbent senator with a legislative record in early learning and childcare plus eight years of prior school-board service.

spending.basis: Record of sponsoring and passing "landmark investments" in childcare and early learning and expanding services for young people.

housing.basis: Cites delivering resources for affordable housing serving seniors, low-income families, and people exiting homelessness — a public-investment approach.

safety.basis: Record spans both poles: voted for law-enforcement pay raises and training as well as funding for drug treatment and recovery.

social.basis: Sponsored medically accurate sexual-health education legislation; names reproductive rights a priority; endorsed by Planned Parenthood.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate from Federal Way, a retired U.S. Air Force Senior Master Sergeant with 24 years of service and four decades of management experience including at Boeing.
highlights: Opposes new income taxes and wants to repeal policies he says drive up fuel and grocery prices, calling for "responsible budgeting instead of higher taxes" [S1][S2]
Supports high academic standards, career and technical education, and parental involvement; says schools should not advance "political agendas" [S1][S2]
No endorsements listed in pamphlet statement or retrieved campaign-site content [S1][S2]

taxes.basis: Repeated explicit opposition to new income taxes and calls for "responsible budgeting instead of higher taxes" across pamphlet and site.

spending.basis: Frames responsible budgeting, rather than new revenue or program expansion, as the answer to state costs.

climate.basis: Wants to repeal policies he says drive up fuel and grocery prices — an affordability-first posture, though he does not name the CCA.

social.basis: Emphasizes parental involvement and says schools should not advance "political agendas."

local-control.basis: Wants local leaders to have decision-making flexibility.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no legislative or elected record, running on military and Boeing management experience to bring "balance" to Olympia.

contests.office_does: The district's single member in the 49-seat Washington State Senate, elected to a four-year term to write and vote on state law and budgets and confirm gubernatorial appointments.
contests.race_blurb: Republican incumbent Phil Fortunato, a senator since 2017 running on spending restraint and Second Amendment rights, faces Democrat Tamara Stramel, a first-time candidate and school-bond organizer. Both advance to the November general election.

candidates.summary: Democratic first-time candidate, a precinct committee officer since 2017 and field organizer for the 2024 Sumner-Bonney Lake school bond, with a background in residential-treatment program management.
highlights: Says schools are "asked to do more with less" and pledges to champion education funding [S1]
Would advocate for the district to be prioritized in the state transportation budget and for rural infrastructure development [S1]
Endorsed by the Washington State Labor Council, AFL-CIO and Pierce County Democrats [S1]

experience.basis: First-time candidate with grassroots organizing background and no elected or legislative record, facing a legislator with roughly 13 years of service.

spending.basis: Argues schools and infrastructure are underfunded and pledges to champion education funding and transportation investment.

candidates.summary: Incumbent Republican state senator since 2017 with prior House service, whose background is in environmental training and consulting and small contracting.
highlights: Attributes the state's financial challenges to "excessive spending and the unchecked growth of government programs" and pledges disciplined use of tax dollars [S1]
Pledges to "continue to stand firmly against efforts to erode our Second Amendment rights" [S1]
Campaign centers on resisting government overreach, safeguarding parental rights, and addressing crime and homelessness [S2]

experience.basis: Senator since 2017 with prior House terms (1998-2000, 2016) — roughly 13 years of cumulative legislative service.

spending.basis: Explicitly attributes state financial challenges to excessive spending and unchecked program growth; pledges disciplined use of tax dollars.

taxes.basis: Runs on tax restraint and disciplined use of tax dollars rather than new revenue, though no specific tax-cut proposals are documented.

safety.basis: Campaign addresses crime and homelessness; his pamphlet cites endorsements from unnamed law-enforcement leaders (self-reported).

social.basis: Campaign lists safeguarding parental rights among its central themes.

contests.office_does: One of 49 members of the state Senate, elected to a four-year term to write state law, adopt state budgets, set tax policy, and confirm gubernatorial appointments.
contests.race_blurb: Two sitting Democratic legislators face each other: Rep. Cindy Ryu, in the House since 2011, is challenging two-term incumbent Sen. Jesse Salomon, calling him 'too moderate,' while Salomon calls himself a 'pragmatic progressive.' Republican first-time candidate Ira McBee runs on partisan balance in this heavily Democratic district.

candidates.summary: Two-term incumbent Democratic senator, former public defender and child welfare prosecutor, who chairs the Senate Local Government Committee and self-describes as a 'pragmatic progressive.'
highlights: Supports a 9.9% 'Millionaires Tax' on incomes over $1 million; passed ESSB 5794 closing corporate tax loopholes and expanded low-income tax rebates [S1][S2][S5]
Emphasizes auditing homelessness spending rather than simply increasing budgets, and addressing the Aurora corridor 'with compassion and accountability' [S1][S4]
Passed ESSB 5611 cutting permitting red tape to speed housing, while his pamphlet also cites capping rent increases and blocking AI-powered rent-setting [S1][S2][S6]
Banned ICE face coverings by law and campaigns on 'Trump-proofing' the state [S1][S2]
Endorsement base of sitting officeholders — legislators, mayors, the Snohomish County Sheriff [S1]

taxes.basis: Champions a 9.9% millionaires tax and has an enacted record of closing corporate tax loopholes while expanding low-income rebates.

spending.basis: Stresses auditing homelessness program spending over budget increases and pairs compassion with accountability, though he also backs major public investment packages.

housing.basis: Genuinely mixed: leads with supply expansion, permitting streamlining, and senior tax relief, but also cites capping rent increases and blocking AI rent-setting.

safety.basis: Mixed posture: calls for connecting people to mental health and addiction treatment, framed 'with compassion and accountability,' and carries the Snohomish County Sheriff's endorsement.

climate.basis: Supports a fracking ban, environmental restoration, and a transportation package his campaign calls among the state's greenest; no explicit carbon-pricing stance in the dossier.

experience.basis: Two-term incumbent with a committee chairmanship, prior JLARC chairmanship, seven years on the Shoreline council, and bills passed each session.

immigration.basis: Passed the ICE face-covering ban and campaigns on 'Trump-proofing' the state against federal overreach.

tech.basis: Blocked AI-powered rent-setting and is working on algorithmic pricing regulation, but has no stated position on data-center subsidies.

candidates.summary: Eight-term Democratic state representative and former Shoreline mayor, giving up her House seat to challenge the incumbent senator from her own party as insufficiently progressive.
highlights: Has backed the capital gains tax and a millionaire's tax; campaigns on a fairer tax system asking the wealthiest to pay their share [S1][S2][S6]
Housing approach leans on mixed-income and social housing plus streamlined permitting and modular construction [S5][S6]

Wants aggressive AI regulation and a comprehensive state privacy law; opposes data-center subsidies, though not the facilities themselves, while supporting AI and quantum development paired with regulation; passed a ban on AI deepfake likeness content [S4][S5][S6]
16 years in the House, chairing the Housing Committee for five years and now Technology, Economic Development & Veterans [S3][S6]
Holds the 32nd District Democrats' endorsement over the incumbent, plus the Washington State Labor Council and WEA [S1][S8]

taxes.basis: Has backed the capital gains tax and a millionaire's tax and campaigns on making the wealthiest pay their share.

spending.basis: Runs on fully funding public education and challenges the incumbent as 'too moderate,' positioning toward public investment over restraint.

housing.basis: Emphasizes mixed-income and social housing, though paired with permitting streamlining and modular construction.

climate.basis: Has backed climate initiatives and wants clean-air legislation targeting working-class communities and communities of color.

experience.basis: Longest legislative tenure in the race: in the House since 2011, five years chairing Housing, current committee chair, with bills signed into law.

tech.basis: Wants aggressive AI regulation and a comprehensive state privacy law, opposes data-center subsidies (though not the facilities themselves) while supporting regulated AI and quantum development, and passed a deepfake-likeness ban.

candidates.summary: Republican first-time candidate, entrepreneur, and international humanitarian worker from Lynnwood/Edmonds, running on partisan balance against one-party control.
highlights: Attributes rising costs to regulatory burdens and government spending rather than revenue shortfalls [S2][S3]
Supports 'responsible budgeting' and reforming spending inefficiency [S1][S3]
Backs rehabilitation for first-time offenders paired with strengthened penalties for repeat offenders [S2][S3]
Supports parental involvement in education, transparency, and expanded skilled-trades pathways [S1][S2]
Has raised under $8,000, roughly one-twentieth of either Democratic opponent [S3][S4]

taxes.basis: Frames affordability as a spending-and-regulation problem, not a revenue shortfall; no specific tax proposals in the dossier.

spending.basis: Runs on responsible budgeting and reforming spending inefficiency as core planks.

safety.basis: Wants to restore safety on transit and in public spaces with stronger repeat-offender penalties, softened by rehabilitation for first-time offenders.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no governing record, running explicitly as balance against entrenched one-party control.

social.basis: Emphasizes parental involvement and transparency in education, with a background in church ministry and homeschool-cooperative instruction.

contests.office_does: One of 49 Washington State Senators, elected to a four-year term to write and vote on state law and budgets covering tax, housing, healthcare, education, and transportation policy.
